alternatives: 3.e5 (Advance Variation) — gains space but locks the center, leading to a ver...

caro-kann.annotation: Black captures, opening the position. This isn't giving up the center — Black already has c6 supporting a potential ...c5 break later.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: Black now has pawns on c6 and e7 with a half-open d-file.
plans: Develop the bishop to f5 before playing ...e6
Use the half-open d-file for pressure later
alternatives: 3...g6 — the Gurgenidze System, a modern approach but less tested at top level

caro-kann.annotation: White recaptures with the knight, placing a powerful centralized piece on e4. This knight controls f6, d6, g5, and c5 — all key squares. It's the reason White played Nc3 instead of other options.
plans: Maintain the strong knight on e4 as long as possible
Develop pieces rapidly and look for attacking chances on the kingside
alternatives: 4.f3 — the Fantasy Variation, gambiting a pawn for a big center

caro-kann.annotation: This is THE move that makes the Caro-Kann special. Black develops the light-squared bishop OUTSIDE the pawn chain before playing ...e6.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: Black will soon play ...e6, creating a solid c6-e6 chain.
plans: Maintain the bishop on f5 as a strong active piece
Play ...e6 and ...Nd7 to complete development solidly
alternatives: 4...Nd7 — the Smyslov variation, keeping more flexibility but the bishop migh...

caro-kann.annotation: White attacks the bishop, forcing a critical decision. The knight retreats to g3 where it eyes f5 and h5 — it's not as dominant as e4, but it gains a tempo and the f5 square might become available l.
plans: Drive the bishop back and seize the f5 square for the knight later
Prepare h4-h5 to further harass the bishop on g6
alternatives: 5.Nc5 — aggressive but premature, Black gets good play after ...e5

caro-kann.annotation: The bishop drops back to g6, staying on the diagonal and maintaining influence over the light squares. This is the standard retreat — the bishop is still active here, controlling d3 and c2.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: No structural change, but the bishop on g6 helps control the light-square complex that Black needs.
plans: Maintain the bishop as a defensive and active piece
Develop with ...Nd7, ...e6, and ...Ngf6 for a solid setup
alternatives: 5...Bg4 — possible but after f3 the bishop has fewer good squares

caro-kann.annotation: A pawn march on the wing! White pushes h4 with a very specific purpose: h5 will trap the bishop on g6 and either force it to the horrible h7 square or win the bishop pair via Bd3.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: The h-pawn advance slightly weakens White's kingside, but the positional gain of misplacing Black'.
plans: Play h5 to trap the bishop on g6 or force it to h7
Follow up with Bd3 to exchange bishops and open the h-file
alternatives: 6.Nf3 — simple development, but lets Black keep the bishop active on g6

caro-kann.annotation: Black prevents Ng5, which would be annoying after h5. The move h6 also creates a flight square on h7 for the bishop after h5.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: The h6 pawn slightly weakens Black's kingside dark squares, but this is a necessary concession.
plans: After h5, retreat the bishop to h7 where it's passive but safe
Complete development with ...Nd7, ...Ngf6, and ...e6
alternatives: 6...h5 — tries to keep the bishop on g6 permanently, but creates a target and...

caro-kann.annotation: White develops the knight to its natural square, controlling e5 and preparing to castle. There's no rush with h5 — White first completes development.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development before pushing h5
Play Bd3 to prepare the bishop exchange on the h-file
alternatives: 7.h5 — immediate, but White's development is lagging; better to develop first

caro-kann.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6. Why d7? Because from d7, the knight supports ...Ngf6 and doesn't block the c-pawn (which is already on c6).
caro-kann.pawnStructure: The knight on d7 keeps Black's pawn structure flexible — c6 remains supported and ...e6 or ...e5 a.
plans: Play ...Ngf6 to complete knight development
Keep the option of ...c5 to challenge White's d4 pawn
alternatives: 7...Nf6 — develops the other knight first, but Nd7 is more flexible in this s...

caro-kann.annotation: Now White pushes h5, and the bishop is banished. This is the payoff of the h4-h5 plan — the bishop on g6 has nowhere good to go.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: The h5 pawn is an advanced outpost that restricts Black's kingside.
plans: Play Bd3 to exchange bishops and open the h-file
Use the h-file and light-square control for a kingside initiative

caro-kann.annotation: Forced retreat. The bishop goes to h7 where it's effectively out of the game — it's blocked by its own pawns and has no active prospects.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: Black's structure remains sound, but the bishop on h7 is a long-term weakness.
plans: Accept the passive bishop and focus on solid development
Aim for ...c5 to generate counterplay against White's d4 pawn

caro-kann.annotation: White offers the bishop trade. This is a key decision — by playing Bd3, White aims to exchange the light-squared bishops via Bxh7.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: After Bxd3 Qxd3, White will have a queen on d3 with pressure down the d-file and along the b1-h7 d.
plans: Exchange bishops to open the h-file
Use the queen on d3 for central and kingside pressure
alternatives: 9.Bd2 — quieter, but misses the chance to exploit the bishop on h7

caro-kann.annotation: Black trades bishops. There's no point avoiding it — the bishop on h7 was doing nothing, so trading it actually improves Black's position slightly.
plans: Complete development with ...Ngf6 and ...e6
Use the freed light squares for piece activity

caro-kann.annotation: White recaptures with the queen, centralizing it beautifully. The queen on d3 is a powerhouse — it controls the d-file, eyes the kingside along the b1-h7 diagonal, and supports potential attacks.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: White has a clear plan now: O-O-O or O-O followed by using the open lines.
plans: Castle and connect rooks
Build an attack using the h-file and central control

caro-kann.annotation: Black solidifies the center with ...e6, creating the classic Caro-Kann pawn triangle on c6-d5(captured)-e6. This locks down d5 and prevents any Nd5 tricks.
caro-kann.pawnStructure: The c6-e6 pawn chain is extremely solid but slightly passive.
plans: Castle kingside and connect rooks
Prepare ...c5 to challenge White's d4 pawn and free the position
alternatives: 10...Ngf6 — develops first, but e6 is needed soon anyway to solidify

pirc-defence.annotation: White seizes the center with the king's pawn, claiming d5 and f5 and opening lines for the bishop and queen.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn. White plans d4 next for a commanding two-pawn center.
plans: Build a d4-e4 pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly behind the strong center
alternatives: 1.d4 — leads to queen's pawn openings, avoids the Pirc entirely

pirc-defence.annotation: The Pirc Defense starts here. Black plays ...d6 instead of challenging the center immediately, saying 'go ahead, build your big center — I'll undermine it later.' This is a hypermodern approach: Bla.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn is modest but flexible. It controls e5 and supports a later ...e5 or ...c5 break d.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure White's center from the flank
Prepare ...e5 or ...c5 to challenge the center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...e5 — the most direct reply, immediately fighting for the center

pirc-defence.annotation: White doesn't hesitate — two pawns in the center, maximum space. The d4-e4 duo controls everything: c5, d5, e5, f5. Against the Pirc, this is practically automatic.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The classical d4-e4 center gives White significant space advantage.
plans: Develop pieces behind the center and prepare a kingside attack
Push f4 to build an even bigger center and threaten f5

pirc-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, pressuring e4 and continuing the hypermodern strategy. The knight on f6 is flexible — it eyes e4 and d5, and doesn't commit to a particular pawn structure yet.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn change. Black keeps maximum flexibility for the structure.
plans: Fianchetto with ...g6 and ...Bg7 next
Castle kingside quickly and then attack the center
alternatives: 2...g6 — transposes to the same thing usually, but Nf6 first keeps options open

pirc-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to protect e4 and control d5. This is the natural developing move — the knight on c3 is the backbone of White's center, defending e4 and preparing to support f4 if White go.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The center is fully supported. Nc3 makes e4 rock-solid and prepares expansive setups.
plans: Play f4 for the Austrian Attack — the most aggressive option
Develop Nf3 and Be2 for a quieter classical setup
alternatives: 3.f3 — preparing a big center with Be3 and Qd2, the Saemisch-like approach

pirc-defence.annotation: The fianchetto begins. Black will place the bishop on g7 where it becomes a monster — aiming straight at White's d4 pawn along the long diagonal.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The g6 pawn creates a shelter for the king and prepares the fianchetto.
plans: Complete the fianchetto with ...Bg7
Castle quickly and then look for ...e5 or ...c5 counterplay

pirc-defence.annotation: The Austrian Attack! White plays f4, building the most aggressive pawn center possible: d4-e4-f4. This is a direct attempt to crush Black before the fianchetto becomes effective.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The d4-e4-f4 pawn trio is powerful but commits White's f-pawn early.
plans: Develop quickly and push e5 to kick the f6 knight and gain more space
Use the f4-f5 advance to attack Black's kingside
alternatives: 4.Nf3 — the Classical system, less aggressive but very solid

pirc-defence.annotation: The bishop takes its throne on g7. This is the most important piece in the Pirc — it pressures d4 along the long diagonal, defends the kingside, and becomes incredibly powerful if Black can ever ope.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn change, but the bishop on g7 adds dynamic pressure to White's d4 pawn, which is the base o.
plans: Castle immediately and get the king safe
Prepare ...c5 or ...e5 to blow open the long diagonal for the bishop

pirc-defence.annotation: White completes minor piece development. The knight on f3 supports d4, controls e5, and prepares castling.
plans: Castle kingside and prepare e5 or f5 advances
Play Bd3 to develop the bishop actively
alternatives: 5.e5 — immediate central advance, very sharp but premature

pirc-defence.annotation: Black castles immediately — getting the king to safety is urgent when White has the Austrian Attack setup.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The king is safe behind the g6-f7-h7 pawns. Black's structure is compact and ready for counterplay.
plans: Play ...c5 to attack White's d4 pawn
Prepare ...e5 to challenge the center head-on

pirc-defence.annotation: White develops the bishop to d3, the most natural square in the Austrian Attack. From d3, the bishop eyes the kingside (particularly h7), supports e4, and can facilitate a later e5 push.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop supports the central pawns and prepares for kingside operations.
plans: Castle and then launch a kingside attack with e5 or f5
Use the d3-h7 diagonal for attacking chances
alternatives: 6.Be2 — safer but less aggressive, the bishop is passive on e2

pirc-defence.annotation: An unusual-looking move, but it's actually very smart. The knight goes to a6 to reroute to c7 and then either e6 or help with ...c5.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Black preserves the ability to play ...c5, which is the most important structural break in the Pirc.
plans: Reroute the knight via c7 to support ...c5 or reach e6
Play ...c5 to challenge the d4 pawn and activate the g7 bishop
alternatives: 6...Nc6 — more natural looking but blocks the c-pawn, making ...c5 impossible

pirc-defence.annotation: White castles, getting the king safe and connecting the rooks. With both sides castled on the same wing, it's a race: White wants to attack on the kingside with e5 or f5, while Black wants to underm.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: No change. White's pawn center remains dominant and the king is safe.
plans: Play e5 to gain more space and kick the f6 knight
Push f5 to pry open Black's kingside

pirc-defence.annotation: The critical counter-strike! Black hits d4 with ...c5, the whole reason the knight went to a6 instead of c6.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The c5 pawn challenges d4 directly. If dxc5 dxc5, Black gets an open d-file and a strong bishop on.
plans: Open the long diagonal for the g7 bishop
Create counterplay on the queenside and in the center
alternatives: 7...e5 — also possible, but ...c5 is the more standard and flexible break

pirc-defence.annotation: White closes the center with d5. This is a committal decision — White locks the structure, which reduces the power of Black's g7 bishop (the long diagonal is blocked) but gains space and a protected.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Closed center with pawns on d5-e4-f4 vs d6-c5.
plans: Attack on the kingside with f5 or e5
Use the space advantage to build pressure
alternatives: 8.dxc5 dxc5 — opens the position, which actually favors Black's bishop on g7

pirc-defence.annotation: The knight completes its journey to c7, exactly as planned from a6. From c7, the knight supports ...b5 and can jump to e8-f6 or a6-b4 as needed.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Black is preparing ...b5 and queenside counterplay.
plans: Play ...a6 and ...b5 for queenside expansion
Prepare ...e6 to challenge the d5 pawn when ready
alternatives: 8...Nc7 is essentially the only move — the knight needs to get off a6

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, claiming central space and opening diagonals for rapid development.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn in the center, controlling d5 and f5.
plans: Build a strong center and develop pieces quickly
Open lines for the queen and bishop

scandinavian-defence.annotation: The Scandinavian Defense! Black immediately strikes at e4 on move one — no preparation, no fianchetto, just a direct punch.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: After exd5 Qxd5, Black will have no center pawns while White has a free hand to build one.
plans: Recapture with the queen and develop quickly despite the early queen sortie
Place pieces actively and aim for a solid, harmonious position
alternatives: 1...c5 (Sicilian) — less committal, creates asymmetry without moving the quee...

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White takes the pawn — there's no reason not to. Now Black faces the defining question of the Scandinavian: recapture with the queen (main line) or play ...Nf6 (the Modern Scandinavian).
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: White has a pawn on d5. If Black takes with the queen, White will get a free tempo with Nc3 develo.
plans: Develop Nc3 with tempo against Black's queen
Build a strong center with d4
alternatives: 2.Nc3 — declining the exchange, rare but playable

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Black recaptures with the queen, entering the main line Scandinavian. Yes, the queen is out early and will get kicked around, but Black gets immediate central presence and avoids any complicated paw.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: Black has no center pawns yet, but the position is completely open.
plans: Retreat the queen to a5 after Nc3, maintaining diagonal pressure
Develop minor pieces quickly: ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6
alternatives: 2...Nf6 (Modern Scandinavian) — gambits the pawn temporarily, avoids the earl...

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White develops with tempo — the knight comes to c3 attacking the queen, which must move again.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn change. White prepares d4 for a strong center.
plans: Follow up with d4 to build a powerful center
Develop rapidly while Black's queen is wandering

scandinavian-defence.annotation: The queen retreats to a5, the most popular square. Why a5? Because it pins the Nc3 along the a5-e1 diagonal (if d4 is played, the knight is pinned to the king after ...Bb4 ideas).
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black will build a center with ...c6 and ...e6.
plans: Develop ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6 for a solid setup
Use the queen's position on a5 to create pin-based threats
alternatives: 3...Qd6 — blocks the d-pawn and the dark-squared bishop

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White builds the ideal center with d4. Now White has the classical d4-e4 (well, e4 was traded) d4 pawn center and a pawn on c3. The center is strong and White has a clear space advantage.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: White has a strong d4 pawn supported by the c3 knight.
plans: Complete development with Nf3, Bc4 or Bd3
Use the space advantage for a middlegame initiative

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the most natural square. It controls d5 and e4, pressuring White's center, and prepares kingside castling.
plans: Continue with ...Bf5 to develop the light-squared bishop actively
Prepare ...e6 and ...Bb4 or ...Be7 for a solid setup

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White mirrors Black's development with the king's knight on f3. It defends d4, controls e5, and prepares to castle.
plans: Develop the bishops and castle quickly
Prepare Bc4 or Bd3 targeting Black's kingside

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Just like in the Caro-Kann, Black gets the light-squared bishop out BEFORE playing ...e6. This is critical — once e6 is played, this bishop would be stuck behind its own pawns forever.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: Black hasn't committed to a pawn structure yet.
plans: Play ...e6 next to solidify, knowing the bishop is already active
Maintain the bishop on f5 as Black's best piece
alternatives: 5...Bg4 — pins the knight but can be challenged by Be2 and h3

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White puts the bishop on c4, targeting the f7 square — the weakest point in Black's camp. The bishop is aggressive here, supporting tactical ideas and creating real threats.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop on c4 creates tactical tension around f7 and d5.
plans: Create threats against f7 and prepare Nd5
Castle and build a strong attacking position
alternatives: 6.Bd3 — targets the f5 bishop directly, aiming for a trade

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Black plays ...e6, solidifying the center and protecting d5 and f7. This was always the plan — get the bishop out first, THEN play ...e6.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: The e6 pawn creates a solid chain with c6 (coming next).
plans: Play ...c6 to complete the solid pawn structure
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White develops the bishop to d2, a sensible square that connects the rooks and prepares queenside castling.
plans: Consider queenside castling for aggressive play
Keep pieces coordinated and prepare to seize the initiative
alternatives: 7.O-O — simple kingside castling, very solid

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Black completes the trademark Scandinavian pawn structure: c6 and e6, a compact fortress. The c6 pawn supports a future ...d5 (if needed) and takes away the d5 square from White's pieces.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: The c6-e6 structure is Black's bread and butter in the Scandinavian.
plans: Develop ...Bb4 or ...Be7 and castle kingside
Look for ...Nd5 or ...c5 breaks in the middlegame

scandinavian-defence.annotation: A powerful knight leap to d5! The knight occupies the ideal central outpost, attacking the queen on a5 and the pawn on e7 simultaneously. From d5, it also eyes c7, b6, and f6 — it's a dominant piece.
plans: Force Black to deal with the strong knight
If ...Nxd5, recapture and maintain central dominance

scandinavian-defence.annotation: The queen retreats to d8, the only safe square. It looks passive, but Black doesn't have a great alternative — Qd6 blocks development, and other squares lose material to knight forks.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The key battle is still around the d5 knight.
plans: Prepare to challenge or trade the d5 knight
Complete development and castle
alternatives: 9...Qb6 — keeps the queen active but exposed to potential Nb4-c2 ideas

scandinavian-defence.annotation: White captures on f6 with check, forcing Black to recapture. This exchange is strategically significant — White trades the dominant knight but damages Black's pawn structure.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: After ...gxf6, Black's kingside pawns are shattered but the bishop pair and open g-file provide co.
plans: Exploit Black's structural weaknesses after the recapture
Use the open lines created by the exchange

scandinavian-defence.annotation: Black recaptures with the g-pawn, which is the more aggressive choice. Yes, the kingside pawns are wrecked — doubled f-pawns and an open g-file.
scandinavian-defence.pawnStructure: Black has doubled f-pawns and an exposed king, but the open g-file and bishop pair offer dynamic c.
plans: Use the open g-file for rook activity
Leverage the bishop pair in an open position
alternatives: 9...exf6 — keeps the structure intact but Black's center is less dynamic and ...

alekhine-defence.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, seizing central space and opening diagonals. This is the move that allows the Alekhine Defense to exist — Black's provocative reply directly targets this pawn.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn in the center. White expects to maintain or expand this center.
plans: Build a strong center and develop pieces behind it
Respond to Black's provocations by advancing the center pawns

alekhine-defence.annotation: The Alekhine Defense — one of the most provocative openings in chess. Black develops a knight to ATTACK e4 on move one, daring White to push e5 and chase it.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn commitment from Black. The idea is to let White advance and then attack the resulting pawn.
plans: Provoke e5, then maneuver the knight to continue provoking advances
Undermine White's overextended center with ...d6, ...c5, or ...f6
alternatives: 1...e5 — classical and solid, doesn't provoke White's center

alekhine-defence.annotation: White accepts the challenge and pushes e5, gaining space and kicking the knight. This is the most natural response — why wouldn't you push a pawn with tempo? White gains central territory, but the e.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: The e5 pawn gains space but is advanced and potentially vulnerable.
plans: Continue advancing to build a massive center with d4, c4
Develop pieces quickly to support the advanced center
alternatives: 2.Nc3 — the Vienna-like approach, solid but lets Black equalize easily

alekhine-defence.annotation: The knight retreats to d5, a powerful central square. From d5, it's stable and well-placed — it controls b4, c3, e3, and f4.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn change yet. Black's strategy depends on attacking White's center with ...d6 soon.
plans: Play ...d6 to challenge the e5 pawn
If White plays c4, retreat to b6 and continue the provocation with ...d6
alternatives: 2...Ng8 — the Brooklyn Defense, retreating all the way back; too passive

alekhine-defence.annotation: White builds the two-pawn center with d4, supporting e5 and claiming dominant central space. This is the standard approach — d4 and e5 together control all the key squares.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: The d4-e5 pawn duo gives White a significant space advantage.
plans: Support the center with c4 to kick the knight and maintain space
Develop pieces behind the strong center

alekhine-defence.annotation: Black strikes at the base of White's center with ...d6. This is the critical moment in the Alekhine — Black directly challenges the e5 pawn.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: After ...d6, the central tension is at its peak.
plans: Challenge and undermine White's center
Develop pieces to pressure e5 and d4
alternatives: 3...d6 could also be played on move 2, but Nd5 first is more flexible

alekhine-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, supporting both d4 and e5 while preparing to castle. This is the Modern Variation — White prioritizes development over the aggressive c4 chase.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: White maintains the d4-e5 center. The position is less aggressive than the Four Pawns Attack but m.
plans: Castle kingside quickly and maintain a solid center
Play Be2 and develop harmoniously
alternatives: 4.c4 (Four Pawns Attack) — the most aggressive, chasing the knight with c4-c5...

alekhine-defence.annotation: Black pins the f3 knight to the queen — a very logical developing move. The bishop on g4 creates real pressure: if White plays Be2, Black can trade bishops and double White's pawns.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change, but the pin on f3 limits White's ability to reinforce the center.
plans: Maintain the pin and develop the rest of the pieces
If White plays Be2, consider ...Bxe2 Qxe2 to damage White's structure
alternatives: 4...Bf5 — develops the bishop but doesn't create the pin pressure

alekhine-defence.annotation: White breaks the pin by developing the bishop to e2. This is the calm, positional approach — the bishop on e2 is modest but solid, it unpins the knight and prepares castling.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No change. The center remains intact and White is ready to castle.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development
Play c4 later to expand on the queenside
alternatives: 5.h3 — forces the bishop to choose, but weakens the kingside slightly

alekhine-defence.annotation: Black plays ...e6, a subtle but important move. It reinforces the pawn on d5, prepares ...Be7 for development, and keeps the position solid.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid e6-d6 chain. The light-squared bishop will need to come back inside the chai.
plans: Develop ...Be7 and castle kingside
Prepare ...f6 or ...c5 to challenge the center
alternatives: 5...dxe5 — opens the center but gives White free development with dxe5

alekhine-defence.annotation: White castles, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. With a strong center and completed kingside development, White has a very comfortable position.
plans: Play c4 to expand and kick the d5 knight
Use the rook on f1 to support f4 if needed

alekhine-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but necessary square. The bishop prepares castling and covers some important dark squares.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No change. Black's setup with e6-d6 and Be7 is solid and prepares to castle.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to challenge the center
alternatives: 6...Bd6 — more ambitious, but the bishop can be hit by c4 and Nc3

alekhine-defence.annotation: White strikes at the powerful d5 knight, forcing it to retreat. The c4 push also grabs more space on the queenside and supports d5 if White ever pushes there.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: White now has pawns on c4, d4, and e5 — a massive pawn center.
plans: Drive the knight away and maintain the space advantage
Develop Nc3 and control the center completely

alekhine-defence.annotation: The knight retreats to b6, the standard square in this line. From b6, the knight eyes c4 and d5, staying relevant despite being pushed back.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The knight on b6 puts pressure on c4 and d5.
plans: Castle and finish development
Play ...Bxf3 at the right moment to double White's pawns
alternatives: 7...Nb4 — aggressive but the knight gets hit by a3

alekhine-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, completing minor piece development. The knight protects e4 (if the pawn were still there) and controls d5 — a vital outpost in this pawn structure.
plans: Build on the space advantage with Be3, Rc1
Play for a d5 or f4 advance in the middlegame

alekhine-defence.annotation: Black castles, finally getting the king to safety. Both sides are now fully developed, and the real battle begins.
alekhine-defence.pawnStructure: The position is stable: White has pawns on c4-d4-e5, Black has e6-d6.
plans: Play ...Bxf3 to double White's pawns and weaken the center
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to break down the pawn chain

philidor-defence.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, claiming the center and opening lines for the bishop and queen. The most popular first move, setting the stage for open games.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn controls d5 and f5. White plans to follow with Nf3 and d4.
plans: Develop quickly with Nf3 and d4 for a classical center
Open lines for rapid piece activity

philidor-defence.annotation: Black mirrors White's approach, creating a symmetrical center. This is the most direct response — Black immediately fights for the same central squares.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical e4 vs e5. The battle for d4 and d5 will determine the middlegame.
plans: Defend e5 and develop pieces naturally
Look for ...d5 break when prepared
alternatives: 1...c5 (Sicilian) — asymmetrical and more combative

philidor-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, attacking e5. The knight controls d4 and helps prepare d4 to open the center.
plans: Attack e5 and prepare d4
Castle kingside quickly

philidor-defence.annotation: The Philidor Defense! Instead of the more common 2...Nc6, Black supports e5 with the d-pawn. The philosophy is defensive: build a solid, compact structure and avoid early tactical complications.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The d6-e5 chain is very solid but slightly cramped.
plans: Develop ...Nf6 and ...Be7, keeping the position solid
Eventually free the position with ...d5 or ...f5
alternatives: 2...Nc6 — the more popular choice, develops a piece and defends e5 actively

philidor-defence.annotation: White immediately challenges the center, which is the right approach against the Philidor.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Central tension between d4/e4 and d6/e5. White's center is aggressive and space-gaining.
plans: Open the center to exploit the space advantage
Develop pieces behind the pawns and attack
alternatives: 3.Bc4 — develops first, less direct but still strong

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the most natural square. It attacks e4, prepares castling, and keeps the position flexible.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The tension in the center remains. Black's knight on f6 pressures e4, adding to the central fight.
plans: Complete development with ...Be7 and ...O-O
Maintain the central tension as long as possible
alternatives: 3...exd4 — releases the tension but opens lines when Black is less developed

philidor-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, supporting e4 and controlling d5. The knight on c3 is essential — it keeps the center intact and prepares for further development.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: White's center is well-supported. The d4-e4 complex is strong and Black must find a way to challen.
plans: Develop Bc4 or Be2 and castle
Maintain central pressure and prepare for middlegame

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6. This is the Hanham Variation approach within the Philidor — the knight on d7 supports e5 and keeps the c-pawn free to advance later with ...c6 or ...c5.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Black's structure remains compact: d6-e5 with knights on f6 and d7.
plans: Play ...Be7 and ...O-O for solid development
Prepare ...c6 followed by ...d5 to free the position
alternatives: 4...Nc6 — more active but blocks the c-pawn and can be hit by d5

philidor-defence.annotation: White puts the bishop on its most aggressive diagonal, targeting f7 — the weakest square in Black's position.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop on c4 creates tactical pressure on f7 and supports central advanc.
plans: Castle and create threats against f7
Prepare d5 or dxe5 to open lines
alternatives: 5.Be2 — solid but less ambitious, doesn't pressure f7

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7 — modest but necessary. The bishop on e7 prepares castling and covers the d6-b4 diagonal.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No change. Black's setup is compact: d6-e5, knights on d7 and f6, bishop on e7.
plans: Castle immediately and get the king safe
Prepare ...c6 and eventually ...d5 to free the position
alternatives: 5...Bb4 — more aggressive pin but can be resolved with a3

philidor-defence.annotation: White castles, completing kingside development. The king is safe, the rook is now on f1 supporting f4 ideas, and White has a clear development advantage.
plans: Play Re1 to support e4 and prepare for central action
Consider dxe5 dxe5 followed by central pressure on the open d-file

philidor-defence.annotation: Black castles, finally getting the king to safety. The position is now a typical Philidor middlegame: White has more space and slightly more active pieces, Black has a solid structure and no weaknes.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Both sides are castled. The d4-e4 vs d6-e5 tension is the defining feature of the position.
plans: Play ...c6 to prepare ...d5
Alternatively play ...Re8 and ...Bf8 to regroup

philidor-defence.annotation: White places the rook on e1, reinforcing the e4 pawn and preparing for central action.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No change. The rook supports the central structure from behind.
plans: Prepare dxe5 to open the d-file if advantageous
Play a4 to gain space on the queenside and restrict ...b5
alternatives: 7.d5 — closes the center, changes the character completely

philidor-defence.annotation: Black plays ...c6, the key preparatory move for the liberating ...d5 push. Without ...c6 first, ...d5 would leave the d5 pawn unsupported and easy to attack.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The c6 pawn completes Black's compact structure.
plans: Play ...d5 to challenge the center and free the pieces
If ...d5 isn't possible yet, play ...Qc7 and ...Rd8 to prepare it

philidor-defence.annotation: White plays a4, a typical move to grab queenside space and prevent ...b5. The pawn on a4 also controls b5, making it harder for Black's knight to reroute via b6.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The a4 pawn claims queenside space. White has a spatial advantage on both wings now, squeezing Bla.
plans: Play a5 to further restrict Black on the queenside
Maintain central tension and look for the right moment to open the position
alternatives: 8.d5 — aggressive central closure, leads to a different type of game

philidor-defence.annotation: Black plays ...b6, preparing to fianchetto the queen's bishop to b7. This is a practical solution to the Philidor's biggest problem — the light-squared bishop has been doing nothing.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Black's b6 creates a slight weakness on c6 and a6, but the benefit of activating the bishop outwei.
plans: Fianchetto with ...Bb7 to activate the light-squared bishop
Prepare ...d5 with all pieces supporting the break
alternatives: 8...Qc7 — prepares ...d5 more directly but the bishop stays passive

petrov-defence.annotation: The King's Pawn opening. We grab the center immediately and open diagonals for our bishop and queen.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn controls d5 and f5 squares.
plans: Develop quickly and fight for central control
Open lines for the king's bishop
alternatives: d4 -- equally strong, leads to closed/semi-closed games

petrov-defence.annotation: Black mirrors us with the most principled reply. By playing e5, Black stakes an equal claim in the center and opens lines for their own dark-squared bishop.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical e4 vs e5 pawn center. Both sides control key central squares.
plans: Maintain central pawn tension
Develop kingside pieces quickly
alternatives: c5 -- the Sicilian, asymmetric and combative

petrov-defence.annotation: We develop the knight to its best square, attacking the e5 pawn immediately. This is the most natural move -- the knight goes to f3 in almost every Open Game.
plans: Attack e5 to gain a tempo advantage
Prepare d4 to build a broad pawn center
alternatives: Bc4 -- the Italian/Bishop's Opening, develops but doesn't pressure e5 directly

petrov-defence.annotation: This is the Petrov Defence. Instead of defending the e5 pawn, Black counterattacks our e4 pawn.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Still symmetrical. Neither side has committed to a specific pawn structure yet.
plans: Counterattack rather than defend -- a philosophical choice by Black
Aim for simplification and equality
alternatives: Nc6 -- the most common defense, keeps more tension

petrov-defence.annotation: We grab the pawn. This is the critical test of the Petrov -- if we don't take, Black just has a comfortable, equal position.
plans: Win a pawn temporarily to disrupt Black's coordination
After Black recaptures, build a strong center with d4
alternatives: Nc3 -- the Three Knights / Four Knights, solid but less testing

petrov-defence.annotation: Black kicks our knight back. This is the Classical Petrov -- the most respected continuation. Black doesn't fall for the trap of Nxe4 immediately.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn creates a small pawn chain pointing toward e5.
plans: Force the knight back, then recapture on e4
Reach a symmetrical position with equal chances
alternatives: Qe7 -- pins the knight but blocks the bishop, slightly awkward

petrov-defence.annotation: We retreat to f3, the natural square. The knight was always coming back here -- the whole Nxe5 adventure was about gaining time and disrupting Black's development sequence.
plans: Play d4 next to establish a classical pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly toward the center
alternatives: Nc4 -- unusual, the knight is offside on c4

petrov-defence.annotation: Now Black takes on e4. The timing is right because our knight is no longer on e5 threatening things. We've reached the main tabiya of the Classical Petrov.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: The e-file is about to become fully open once we play d4 and Black's knight moves.
plans: Win the pawn back with d4 hitting the d6 pawn
Use the semi-open e-file for rook activity
alternatives: Be7 -- too passive, lets White keep a comfortable edge after d4

petrov-defence.annotation: This is the key move. We establish a classical pawn center with d4, and we're hitting Black's d6 pawn which was supporting the knight.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: White has a broad d4 center. Black needs to equalize by challenging it or completing development.
plans: Develop Bd3 to pressure the e4 knight
Castle quickly and use the open center to our advantage
alternatives: Bd3 -- developing first, but d4 is more direct and principled

petrov-defence.annotation: Black solidifies. The pawn on d5 supports the pawn on e4 and creates a symmetrical pawn structure. This is the hallmark of the Petrov -- both sides have a pawn on d4/d5, and the position is balanced.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. The center is locked and neither side has a pawn majority yet.
plans: Maintain the solid d5 outpost for pieces
Develop smoothly and prepare to challenge White's initiative
alternatives: Be7 -- allows Bd3 with tempo, less accurate

petrov-defence.annotation: We develop the bishop to its ideal diagonal, directly targeting the knight on e4.
plans: Pressure the e4 knight and the h7 square
Castle kingside quickly
alternatives: Qe2 -- pins the knight but blocks the bishop development

petrov-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square. The knight on c6 puts pressure on d4, our center pawn, and supports the knight on e4 indirectly.
plans: Pressure the d4 pawn
Prepare Be7 and castle kingside
alternatives: Be7 -- equally good, a matter of move order

petrov-defence.annotation: We castle immediately. King safety first. In the Petrov, the center is semi-open and things can get tactical quickly, so getting the king tucked away is essential.
plans: Activate the f1 rook on the e-file
Prepare Re1 to increase central pressure
alternatives: c4 -- aggressive but premature before castling

petrov-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop modestly to e7. This is very typical of the Petrov -- Black isn't trying to be flashy.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black is completing development before making pawn commitments.
plans: Castle kingside and reach a safe, solid position
Consider Bf6 later to put pressure on d4
alternatives: Bd6 -- more active but blocks the d-pawn and can be hit by c4

petrov-defence.annotation: Now we strike at the center. The c4 push challenges Black's d5 pawn directly and is the main way to create tension in the Petrov.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: We're attacking the d5 base. If dxc4, Bxc4 gives us the open d-file and bishop pair ideas.
plans: Open the position to exploit our lead in development
Create pressure against d5 or gain the bishop pair
alternatives: Re1 -- solid, improves the rook first

petrov-defence.annotation: Black plays actively, jumping to b4 to target our d3 bishop. The idea is to force the bishop to move and potentially win time. The knight on b4 also eyes the c2 and d3 squares.
plans: Harass the Bd3 and gain time
Potentially land the knight on d3 or c2 if White is careless
alternatives: Nf6 -- retreats to safety but concedes ground

petrov-defence.annotation: We retreat the bishop to e2 -- a modest but correct square. The bishop on e2 stays on a useful diagonal and doesn't block anything. We could have gone to c2 or e4, but e2 is the most flexible.
plans: Maintain pressure with the c4 pawn
Prepare a3 to kick the knight from b4 if needed
alternatives: Bc2 -- keeps the bishop active but the knight on b4 is annoying there

petrov-defence.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety. This is the right moment -- the pieces are developed enough and the king needs to be out of the center before the position opens further.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: The c4 vs d5 tension remains the key structural question.
plans: Complete development and prepare for the middlegame
The knight on b4 may need to find a better home soon
alternatives: Bf5 -- develops the bishop before castling, but king safety takes priority

queens-gambit.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately claiming central space and opening the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. Unlike 1.e4, this move creates a solid pawn chain that's harder to undermine.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Single d4 pawn controls e5 and c5. White aims to expand with c4 next.
plans: Follow up with c4 to build an ideal pawn center
Develop pieces behind the pawn chain for a strong middlegame

queens-gambit.annotation: Black fights for the center immediately by meeting d4 with d5. This is the most principled response — Black stakes an equal claim in the center and prevents White from getting a two-pawn ideal cente.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. The central tension will define the opening battle.
plans: Maintain the d5 pawn and develop pieces naturally
Look for ...c5 to challenge White's center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...Nf6 (Indian Defenses) — flexible, avoids committing the d-pawn early

queens-gambit.annotation: This is the Queen's Gambit — White offers the c4 pawn to lure Black's d5 pawn away from the center. It's not a real gambit because Black can't hold the pawn comfortably.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: White has pawns on d4 and c4, pressuring Black's d5 from the side.
plans: If Black takes on c4, recapture or play e4 for a big center
If Black holds d5, build pressure with Nc3 and prepare to exchange on d5
alternatives: 2.Nf3 — solid development, keeps options open

queens-gambit.annotation: Black declines the gambit and supports d5 solidly with e6. This is the Queen's Gambit Declined — the most classical and reliable response.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's pawn chain d5-e6 is solid but passive. The c8 bishop is blocked.
plans: Develop pieces and maintain the solid d5-e6 structure
Eventually break with ...c5 or ...e5 to free the position
alternatives: 2...dxc4 (Queen's Gambit Accepted) — gives up the center but gains time

queens-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to its best square, adding another attacker to d5. Now the c4 pawn and the c3 knight both pressure d5, and Black has to decide how to handle this tension.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 vs Black's d5+e6. The pressure on d5 is mounting with two pieces and a pawn targetin.
plans: Increase pressure on d5 and prepare cxd5 at the right moment
Prepare e4 after the d5 tension resolves in White's favor

queens-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, defending d5 and controlling e4. This is standard developing chess — every piece should go to its best square.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: The d5-e6 chain remains solid. Black's minor pieces are developing naturally.
plans: Continue development with ...Be7 and ...O-O
Maintain d5 and look for counterplay with ...c5 or ...dxc4

queens-gambit.annotation: White captures on d5, entering the Exchange Variation of the QGD. This is a strategic decision — by exchanging pawns, White creates a symmetrical pawn structure but with a key advantage: the minorit.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: After the exchange, White has a d4 pawn vs Black's e6.
plans: Launch the minority attack with b4-b5 to create queenside weaknesses
Develop the dark-squared bishop actively before playing e3
alternatives: 5.Bg5 (Classical) — maintains the tension and pins the knight

queens-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the e-pawn, which is virtually forced. Taking with the knight would lose central presence. Now the pawn structure is symmetrical — both sides have a d-pawn supported by pieces.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5 with open e-file. Black's d5 pawn is the long-term target — it can become wea.
plans: Develop the light bishop actively to f5 or g4 before White restricts it
Use the open e-file and aim for piece activity to compensate for the isolated...

queens-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop aggressively before blocking it in with e3. This is the most precise move order — you always want to develop the dark-squared bishop BEFORE playing e3, because e3 locks it.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4/d5. The bishop on g5 adds pressure to the kingside and the d5 pawn indirectly by ta.
plans: Maintain the pin on f6 to pressure Black's d5 pawn
If Black plays ...Be7, exchange on e7 to weaken Black's kingside or double pawns

queens-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, which is the most natural and solid response to the Bg5 pin. The bishop unpins the knight and prepares castling.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. Black is focused on completing development and getting the king to safety.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Consider ...Nbd7 to reroute the knight and support the center
alternatives: 6...h6 — forces the bishop to declare its intentions but weakens the kingside...

queens-gambit.annotation: Now that the dark-squared bishop is already developed to g5, White plays e3 to solidify the d4 pawn and open the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 chain is very solid. The light-squared bishop will develop to d3, targeting the king.
plans: Develop the light bishop to d3 aiming at h7
Castle kingside and then start the minority attack with Rab1 and b4

queens-gambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. This is the right time to castle — the bishop is developed, the knight is on f6, and there's nothing more urgent.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The king is safe on g8 with the f7-g7-h7 pawn shield intact.
plans: Develop the remaining minor pieces with ...Nbd7 and ...Re8
Prepare ...c6 to support d5 and deny White's knight access to b5

queens-gambit.annotation: White places the bishop on its ideal diagonal, pointing straight at h7. The Bd3 is a monster in the Exchange QGD — it supports a potential kingside attack and eyes the h7 pawn, which is a classic ta.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 provides a solid base for the bishop.
plans: Play Qc2 to create the battery on the b1-h7 diagonal
Combine the kingside battery with the queenside minority attack for pressure ...

queens-gambit.annotation: Black develops the last minor piece to d7, where it supports the knight on f6 and keeps options flexible.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. Black is completing development before committing to a plan.
plans: Play ...c6 to support d5 and create a solid queenside structure
Reroute the knight via Nf8-e6 or Nb6 depending on White's setup
alternatives: 8...c6 — immediately supporting d5, but the knight still needs to develop

queens-gambit.annotation: White develops the kingside knight to its natural square, controlling e5 and preparing to castle. The knight on f3 is perfectly placed — it supports d4, eyes e5, and helps with kingside castling.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. White's setup is nearly complete — just needs to castle and start the minorit.
plans: Castle kingside next to complete development
Then play Rab1, a3, and b4 to begin the minority attack

queens-gambit.annotation: Black places the rook on the semi-open e-file, which is the most logical square for it. The rook on e8 eyes the e3 pawn and supports a potential ...e5 break in the future.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The open e-file becomes a point of contention between both sides.
plans: Prepare ...Nf8-e6 to challenge the bishop on g5
Keep the option of ...e5 alive in the right circumstances

queens-gambit.annotation: White completes the famous Bd3+Qc2 battery on the b1-h7 diagonal. This is one of the most important setups in the Exchange QGD.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Structure unchanged. The Bd3+Qc2 battery creates latent threats against Black's kingside.
plans: Castle and then launch the minority attack with Rab1, a3, b4-b5
Keep the kingside threats alive — if Black plays ...g6, the battery becomes l...

queens-gambit.annotation: Black plays the critical move ...c6, solidifying the d5 pawn once and for all. This is Black's main defensive idea in the Exchange QGD — the c6 pawn makes d5 rock-solid and also prevents any Nb5 ideas.
queens-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has the c6-d5 pawn chain. This is very solid but slightly passive.
plans: Hold the solid position and look for piece activity
Consider ...Ne4 to exchange pieces and ease the pressure
alternatives: 10...Nf8 — rerouting the knight for kingside defense first

london-system.annotation: We open with the queen's pawn, staking a claim in the center and launching the London System.
london-system.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4. White aims to build a solid d4-e3 pawn duo rather than an aggressive d4.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop to f4 before playing e3
Build a solid pawn triangle with d4-e3-c3
alternatives: Nf3 — a flexible move that can also lead to the London after Bf4

london-system.annotation: Black mirrors our central claim, establishing a symmetrical pawn tension in the center. This is the most classical response, leading to a solid Queen's Pawn structure.
london-system.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5 central pawns. Neither side has committed to a pawn exchange yet, maintaining.
plans: Maintain the d4-d5 tension rather than exchanging
Develop the bishop to f4 before locking it in with e3

london-system.annotation: The signature move of the London System! We develop the dark-squared bishop BEFORE playing e3, because after e3 this bishop would be locked behind its own pawns.
london-system.pawnStructure: Still d4 vs d5. The key principle: always develop Bf4 before playing e3, or the bishop gets buried.
plans: Support e5 control and prevent Black from freeing their position with ...e5
After e3-Bd3, aim the bishop battery at Black's kingside
alternatives: Nf3 — also good, but risks Black playing ...Bf5 to contest the diagonal

london-system.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its most natural square, attacking the e4 square and preparing kingside castling. This is a standard developing move that keeps all of Black's options open.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change. The d4-d5 tension remains.
plans: Continue development with ...e6 and ...Be7 or ...Bd6
Potentially break with ...c5 to challenge White's center

london-system.annotation: We solidify our pawn center by creating the famous London pawn triangle. The pawn on e3 supports d4, secures the Bf4's retreat path, and opens the f1-a6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop.
london-system.pawnStructure: The d4-e3 pawn duo forms a solid triangle with f2.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop to d3, aiming at Black's kingside
Play Nf3, Nbd2, c3 to complete development

london-system.annotation: Black solidifies the d5 pawn and opens the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This creates a solid Stonewall-like pawn chain on d5-e6 that controls the center well.
london-system.pawnStructure: Black has d5-e6, a solid but slightly passive structure.
plans: Try to activate the light-squared bishop via ...b6 and ...Bb7 or ...Ba6
Challenge White's center with ...c5

london-system.annotation: We develop the king's knight to its ideal square, reinforcing control of e5 and d4 while preparing to castle.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change. White maintains the d4-e3 structure with pieces supporting it.
plans: Castle kingside next
Develop Bd3 and Nbd2 to complete the London setup
alternatives: Nd2 — less common but avoids blocking the f-pawn; however, Nf3 is more natural

london-system.annotation: Black strikes at our center with the most principled break. By attacking d4, Black aims to dissolve our central pawn chain and open lines for their pieces, especially the dark-squared bishop.
london-system.pawnStructure: Black challenges the d4-e3 chain with ...c5.
plans: Strike back with ...cxd4 to open the c-file
Develop pieces actively and target White's center

london-system.annotation: We reinforce d4 with the c-pawn, creating an ironclad pawn center that is very hard to break down. The c3 pawn completes our pawn triangle (c3-d4-e3) and takes all pressure off our center.
london-system.pawnStructure: The c3-d4-e3 pawn triangle is the hallmark London structure.
plans: Complete development with Bd3, Nbd2, and O-O
After full development, prepare e3-e4 to seize more space
alternatives: dxc5 — captures but gives up the center; not in the London spirit

london-system.annotation: Black develops the queen's knight to its natural square, adding another attacker to the d4 pawn and preparing to increase central pressure.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change. Black has two pieces and the c5 pawn targeting d4, but White's pawn triangle holds.
plans: Increase pressure on d4 with ...Qb6 or ...Rc8
Prepare ...e5 to strike in the center

london-system.annotation: We develop the queen's knight to d2 rather than c3, which is a key London System idea.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change. White's solid c3-d4-e3 triangle remains intact.
plans: Develop bishop to d3 next, completing the London setup
Reroute Nd2 to e5 if the square is available
alternatives: Bd3 — also excellent; developing order between Nbd2 and Bd3 is flexible

london-system.annotation: Black develops the dark-squared bishop to d6, directly challenging our London bishop on f4. This is one of the most critical moments in the London System: Black offers a trade of dark-squared bishops.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change yet, but if Bxd6 Qxd6 occurs, Black keeps a flexible structure.
plans: Exchange on f4 to try to play ...e5 with tempo
Use Bd6 to support central expansion with ...e5

london-system.annotation: We retreat the bishop to g3 rather than allowing the exchange on d6. This is the modern main line approach in the London.
london-system.pawnStructure: No change. White's structure remains solid.
plans: Complete development with Bd3 and O-O
The Bg3 supports e5 control and a potential h4-h5 kingside pawn storm
alternatives: Bxd6 — trading is simpler but gives Black easy development; Bg3 keeps more te...

london-system.annotation: Black castles kingside, tucking the king into safety and connecting the rooks. This is a natural and necessary move.
london-system.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black's king is now on g8 behind the f7-g7-h7 pawns.
plans: Use the rooks on the central files
Play ...e5 to challenge the center

london-system.annotation: We place our light-squared bishop on its ideal diagonal, aiming straight at Black's kingside and the critical h7 pawn.
london-system.pawnStructure: White's c3-d4-e3 triangle is complete with all pieces developed.
plans: Castle kingside on the next move
After castling, prepare e3-e4 to open the center with a space advantage

london-system.annotation: Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to b7, solving the problem of the hemmed-in c8 bishop that was locked behind the e6 pawn.
london-system.pawnStructure: Black adds b6, preparing a queenside fianchetto.
plans: Develop the bishop to b7 for central pressure
Play ...Bb7 followed by ...Qe7 and ...Rc8 or ...e5
alternatives: ...Qe7 — preparing ...e5 directly without spending time on the fianchetto

catalan-opening.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, claiming central space and controlling the e5 and c5 squares.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: A single central pawn on d4 controls dark squares.
plans: Follow up with c4 to form the Queen's Gambit pawn center
Develop knights to f3 and prepare a fianchetto with g3

catalan-opening.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its most natural square, immediately contesting White's control of the center. The knight attacks d5 and e4, discouraging White from playing e4 easily.
plans: Control e4 and d5 from f6
Prepare kingside castling

catalan-opening.annotation: White forms the classic Queen's Gambit pawn duo. The c4 pawn strikes at d5, fighting for the center alongside d4. Together these two pawns control the four key central squares.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: The c4-d4 duo is the backbone of the Queen's Gambit complex.
plans: Challenge Black's center if ...d5 comes
Prepare the Catalan fianchetto with g3 and Bg2

catalan-opening.annotation: Black supports a future ...d5 advance with the e-pawn, choosing a solid setup. This is the hallmark of the Queen's Gambit Declined family — Black builds a sturdy pawn chain but accepts a slightly cr.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: Black's e6 pawn supports d5 solidly but locks in the light-squared bishop on c8.
plans: Support ...d5 to challenge White's center
Develop the dark-squared bishop to e7 and castle kingside
alternatives: g6 — the King's Indian Defense, a more combative approach

catalan-opening.annotation: The defining move of the Catalan! White prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop to g2, where it will become a monster on the long diagonal.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: White commits to a fianchetto structure. The g3 pawn slightly weakens the dark squares around the.
plans: Fianchetto with Bg2, aiming at d5 and the long diagonal
Build slow positional pressure on Black's queenside

catalan-opening.annotation: Black occupies the center with ...d5, establishing a foothold. This is the principled response — Black refuses to let White dominate d5 unopposed. However, this pawn is now a target.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: A classic QGD tension: White's c4 attacks Black's d5.
plans: Maintain the d5 pawn as a central anchor
Decide when to release tension with ...dxc4
alternatives: Bb4+ — the Bogo-Indian, pinning the knight if it were on c3

catalan-opening.annotation: The Catalan bishop takes its throne on g2. From here it radiates power along the entire a8-h1 diagonal, pressuring d5 and looking deep into Black's queenside.
plans: Maintain long-diagonal pressure on d5 and b7
Support a future e4 break to open the center

catalan-opening.annotation: Black develops the bishop modestly to e7, preparing kingside castling. While not the most active square, Be7 is solid — it avoids any tactical tricks and keeps the position flexible.
plans: Complete kingside development and castle quickly
Keep the position solid while deciding on a plan
alternatives: Bb4+ — the Vienna Variation, more ambitious but allows White to recapture the...

catalan-opening.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, controlling e5 and d4 while preparing to castle. The knight on f3 supports the center harmoniously and avoids blocking the Catalan bishop's diagonal.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development
Prepare for the central battle after Black decides on a pawn structure

catalan-opening.annotation: Black castles kingside, tucking the king to safety. This is essential before the center opens — the king needs to be sheltered before the pawn tension is resolved.
plans: Complete development and address the light-squared bishop
Decide on a queenside plan: ...b6 and ...Bb7, or ...dxc4 and ...b5

catalan-opening.annotation: White castles, completing the kingside setup. Both kings are now safe, and the real strategic battle begins.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: The central tension c4 vs d5 remains unresolved.
plans: Play Qc2 or Qd3 to connect rooks and add pressure to d5
Wait for Black to commit before choosing a plan

catalan-opening.annotation: Black captures the c4 pawn, entering the Open Catalan. This is a critical decision — Black grabs a pawn but opens the long diagonal for White's bishop on g2, which now stares directly at the queenside.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: The center is now asymmetric: White has a lone d4 pawn against Black's e6.
plans: Try to hold the c4 pawn with ...a6 and ...b5
Develop the light-squared bishop and consolidate
alternatives: Nbd7 — keeping the tension, entering the Closed Catalan

catalan-opening.annotation: White places the queen on c2, immediately targeting the c4 pawn and preparing to recapture it. The queen also eyes the h7 pawn indirectly and connects the rooks.
plans: Recapture on c4 at the right moment
Support a future e4 advance to open the center

catalan-opening.annotation: Black plays ...a6, preparing to defend the extra c4 pawn with ...b5. This is the classic method in the Open Catalan — Black tries to hold the pawn and develop the light-squared bishop to b7, solving.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: Black prepares a queenside pawn chain with ...a6 and ...b5 to protect c4.
plans: Follow up with ...b5 to defend c4
Develop the bishop to b7 via the b-file
alternatives: b5 — immediately defending, but a6 first is more flexible

catalan-opening.annotation: White recaptures the pawn, restoring material equality. The queen is actively placed on c4, where it pressures a6, eyes the e6 pawn, and keeps the c-file influence.
plans: Maintain pressure on the queenside light squares
Develop Nc3 or Nbd2 and place rooks on c1 and d1

catalan-opening.annotation: Black pushes ...b5, chasing the queen away and gaining space on the queenside. This is thematic — Black wants to develop the bishop to b7 where it challenges the long diagonal.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: Black's queenside pawn majority (a6-b5) gains space but creates holes on c5 and a5.
plans: Develop the bishop to b7 on the long diagonal
Expand on the queenside and activate the rooks

catalan-opening.annotation: White retreats the queen to c2, a familiar and excellent square. The queen stays on the c-file, keeps the h7 threat alive, and clears c4 for a potential knight outpost.
plans: Develop the knight to c3 or d2 and place rooks on c1 and d1
Prepare e4 to open the center when the time is right

catalan-opening.annotation: Black finally solves the light-squared bishop problem by developing it to b7. The bishop now contests the long diagonal against White's Catalan bishop on g2 — a critical battle in this opening.
catalan-opening.pawnStructure: Both sides have completed development. White's d4 pawn and Black's e6 pawn define a relatively sta.
plans: Contest the a8-h1 diagonal against White's Catalan bishop
Look for ...c5 to challenge the d4 pawn and free the position

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, staking a claim in the center and keeping all options open.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: A single central pawn on d4 controls e5 and c5. White keeps the c-pawn and e-pawn flexible for now.
plans: Follow up with Bg5 to enter the Trompowsky Attack
Control the dark squares in the center

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its most natural square, attacking the e4 square and preparing kingside development.
plans: Control e4 and prepare ...d5 or ...g6
Develop naturally toward kingside castling

trompowsky-attack.annotation: The Trompowsky Attack! White develops the bishop to g5 before playing Nf3, immediately putting pressure on the f6 knight.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The pawn structure is still fluid. White's plan with Bg5 is to provoke structural weaknesses in Bl.
plans: Pressure the f6 knight — trade it to double Black's pawns, or force it to move
Avoid main-line theory and steer the game into less familiar territory
alternatives: Nf3 — the classical approach, entering the main lines of d4 theory

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black counterattacks the bishop immediately by advancing the knight to e4. This is one of the most principled responses — rather than passively defending, Black forces the bishop to make a decision.
plans: Challenge the Bg5 directly and force it to retreat or clarify
Gain time and prepare ...d5 with a solid center
alternatives: d5 — a solid, classical approach keeping the knight on f6

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White retreats the bishop to f4, maintaining it on a strong diagonal where it eyes the b8-h2 line. From f4 the bishop supports e3 and keeps pressure on the c7 and d6 squares.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: No pawn changes yet. White is content to develop pieces before committing the pawn structure.
plans: Keep the bishop active on the b8-h2 diagonal
Prepare e3 and Bd3 for a harmonious development
alternatives: Bh4 — keeping the pin on the f6 square, but the knight is already on e4

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black establishes a strong central pawn on d5, claiming space and providing a solid foundation. The pawn on e4 is now well-supported by the d5 pawn.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: Black's d5 pawn forms a solid central anchor.
plans: Anchor the center with ...d5 and develop naturally
Prepare ...c5 to challenge White's d4 pawn

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White plays a solid, restrained move with e3, supporting the d4 pawn and opening the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 pawn chain is sturdy but modest.
plans: Develop Bd3 aiming at the kingside
Prepare Nf3 and short castling for a solid setup

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black immediately strikes at White's center with ...c5, the most active plan. This challenges the d4 pawn and fights for central space. If White captures, Black recaptures with good piece activity.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The c5 pawn challenges d4, creating central tension.
plans: Challenge d4 and open lines for piece development
Prepare ...Nc6 to add pressure on d4

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White develops the bishop to its ideal square, targeting the h7 pawn and the kingside in general.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: White maintains the d4-e3 chain and lets Black decide how to resolve the central tension.
plans: Aim the bishop at h7 for future kingside attacks
Complete development with Nf3, O-O, and build pressure

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black reroutes the knight from e4 to d6, where it defends the f5 square and prepares to reroute to f5 — an excellent outpost. The knight on d6 is temporarily awkward but has a clear destination.
plans: Reroute the knight to f5, a powerful outpost
Support the center from d6 and prepare ...Be7 and castling

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White develops the knight to its best square, controlling e5 and d4 while preparing to castle. The Nf3 also supports the d4 pawn, which is under pressure from Black's c5.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The central tension between d4 and c5 remains.
plans: Castle kingside quickly to safeguard the king
Maintain central control and prepare c3 to reinforce d4

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black develops the second knight to c6, adding pressure on the d4 pawn and preparing to complete kingside development.
plans: Increase pressure on d4 alongside the c5 pawn
Prepare ...Be7 and ...O-O to complete development

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White castles kingside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. This is an important moment — with pieces well-developed (Bd3, Bf4, Nf3), White can now focus on middlegame plans.
plans: Begin middlegame operations with c3 to solidify the center
Place rooks on e1 and/or d1 for central pressure

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black completes the knight maneuver Ne4-d6-f5, landing on a superb outpost. The knight on f5 is beautifully centralized, pressuring d4 and e3, and it cannot easily be driven away by pawns.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The pawn structure remains in tension. The Nf5 puts additional indirect pressure on e3 and d4, mak.
plans: Use the Nf5 as a permanent outpost to pressure White's center
Complete development with ...Be7 and ...O-O

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White reinforces the d4 pawn with c3, creating a rock-solid center. This is an essential positional move — with Black's pieces bearing down on d4, White needs this extra support.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: White now has a c3-d4-e3 pawn chain, extremely solid and hard to break down.
plans: Solidify the center completely
Develop Nbd2 to support the center and prepare e4 if possible
alternatives: Re1 — developing a rook, but d4 needs immediate reinforcement

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black plays ...e6, solidifying the d5 pawn and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop to e7. The pawn on e6 also supports the Nf5 indirectly by controlling d5.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: Black's d5-e6 pawn chain is solid and controls the center well.
plans: Develop ...Be7 and castle kingside
Look for a break with ...f6 or ...b5 later to activate the position

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White develops the knight to d2, the only route available since c3 blocks Nc3. From d2 the knight supports the center and can reroute to e5 via f3 (if the other knight moves) or push e4 by supportin.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The c3-d4-e3 chain is fully supported. White's pieces are harmoniously placed behind the center.
plans: Prepare e4 to open the center when the time is right
Reroute the Nd2 to support e4 or maneuver to b3/c4

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but practical square. The bishop supports kingside castling and keeps the position flexible.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Keep the position solid and look for a favorable moment to strike

trompowsky-attack.annotation: White places the queen on c2, a multi-purpose square. The queen supports a future e4 break, adds pressure along the c-file, and connects to the Bd3 to create a battery aimed at h7.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: No structural change. White is building up piece pressure before committing to pawn breaks.
plans: Create the Qc2 + Bd3 battery targeting h7 after Black castles
Prepare the e4 central break with full piece support

trompowsky-attack.annotation: Black castles kingside, completing development and getting the king to safety. Both sides have now castled and the middlegame battle begins.
trompowsky-attack.pawnStructure: The central tension (c5 vs d4) and the pawn chains (c3-d4-e3 for White, d5-e6 for Black) define th.
plans: Seek counterplay with ...b5 or ...a5 on the queenside
Maintain the Nf5 outpost and keep the center closed

qgd.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center. Unlike 1.e4, this move keeps things a bit more positional from the start -- the pawn is already defended by the queen, s.
plans: Build a broad pawn center with c4
Control the d5 and e5 squares
alternatives: e4 -- the other main first move, leading to more open games

qgd.annotation: Black immediately plants a pawn in the center to contest White's control of d4 and e5.
plans: Maintain the d5 strongpoint
Develop pieces behind a solid pawn chain
alternatives: Nf6 -- the Indian Defense approach, more flexible but concedes the center tem...

qgd.annotation: This is the Queen's Gambit -- White offers the c-pawn to lure Black's d-pawn away from the center. It's not a real gambit because if Black takes, White gets it back easily.
qgd.pawnStructure: White has pawns on d4 and c4 vs Black's d5 -- the classic QG tension
plans: Challenge d5 to eventually play e4
Open the c-file for the rook after the pawn exchange
alternatives: Nf3 -- a quieter approach that delays the central tension

qgd.annotation: Black declines the gambit and supports d5 with the e-pawn. This is the Queen's Gambit Declined -- super solid but it locks in the c8-bishop behind the pawn chain.
qgd.pawnStructure: Black's e6-d5 chain is rock solid but the light-squared bishop is hemmed in
plans: Maintain d5, develop kingside, solve the c8-bishop problem
alternatives: dxc4 -- the Queen's Gambit Accepted, giving up the center for active piece play

qgd.annotation: White develops the knight to its most natural square, adding a third attacker to d5. The knight on c3 is incredibly well-placed -- it pressures d5 and e4, the two most important central squares.
plans: Increase pressure on d5
Prepare e4 push when the time is right
alternatives: Nf3 -- also strong, often transposing

qgd.annotation: Black develops the knight to its best square, defending d5 a second time while also controlling e4. This is the most natural developing move -- the knight belongs on f6 in almost every QGD line.
plans: Defend d5, prepare O-O
Eventually break with either ...c5 or ...dxc4 followed by ...c5
alternatives: Nbd7 -- a Lasker-style move order, but slightly passive

qgd.annotation: White pins the knight to the queen -- this is the most principled and aggressive setup in the QGD.
plans: Pin the Nf6 to increase pressure on d5
Threaten Bxf6 to double Black's pawns or force concessions
alternatives: Bf4 -- quieter, avoids the pin but less pressure on d5

qgd.annotation: Black breaks the pin in the most solid way. The bishop on e7 is modest but functional -- it unpins the knight, prepares castling, and keeps the position stable.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Keep the position solid, look for ...dxc4 and ...c5 breaks later
alternatives: Bb4 -- the Ragozin, pinning Nc3 and creating tactical complications

qgd.annotation: White supports d4 and opens the diagonal for the f1-bishop. This is a very solid choice -- White isn't in a rush.
qgd.pawnStructure: White's d4-e3 chain is very stable, supporting a slow buildup
plans: Develop Bd3, complete development, prepare for a kingside or central initiative
alternatives: Rc1 -- immediately preparing for the c-file

qgd.annotation: Black castles to safety immediately, which is the priority in the QGD. The king on g8 is tucked away, and the rook comes to f8 where it can support a future ...f5 break or just hold the f-file.
plans: Complete development with ...Nbd7
Look for the right moment to play ...dxc4 or ...c5

qgd.annotation: White develops the last minor piece to its ideal square. The knight on f3 supports d4, controls e5, and completes the kingside development.
plans: Castle or play Bd3 next
Build up before choosing a plan based on Black's setup
alternatives: Bd3 -- also good, getting the bishop active before the knight

qgd.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6 -- this is a key QGD decision. On d7, the knight supports the e5 and c5 breaks and doesn't block the c-pawn.
plans: Prepare ...c5 or ...c6 break
Reroute Nd7 to f8, e5, or b6 depending on the position
alternatives: c6 -- immediately solidifying d5, but the knight hasn't gone anywhere yet

qgd.annotation: White puts the rook on the semi-open file it's going to need. After Black eventually plays ...dxc4, the c-file opens and this rook will be perfectly placed.
plans: Anticipate ...dxc4, control the c-file
Support the c4 pawn and Nc3
alternatives: Bd3 -- developing the bishop is also logical

qgd.annotation: Black plays ...c6 to give the d5 pawn extra support and prepare a potential ...dxc4 followed by a queenside expansion with ...b5. This is the Tartakower-Makogonov-Bondarevsky system idea.
qgd.pawnStructure: Black's c6-d5-e6 triangle is extremely solid but somewhat passive
plans: Play ...dxc4 at the right moment, then ...Nd5 targeting the pin
Prepare ...b5 for queenside counterplay
alternatives: b6 -- fianchettoing the bishop to solve the light-square problem

qgd.annotation: White places the bishop on the best diagonal, aiming at the kingside through h7. The bishop on d3 is a monster in many QGD lines -- it creates latent threats against the Black king once the position.
plans: Eye h7 for potential kingside attacks
Support e4 break when the time is right
alternatives: Bxc4 -- recapturing on c4 if it's taken, but Bd3 is stronger when the tension...

qgd.annotation: Black finally captures on c4, releasing the central tension. This is well-timed because the knight is ready to come to d5 on the next move, where it's incredibly strong.
qgd.pawnStructure: The center opens up -- White gets a d4 isolani or e4 expansion opportunity
plans: Follow up with ...Nd5, challenging the pin and centralizing powerfully
alternatives: Nd5 -- immediately going for the knight jump, but the c4 pawn capture first i...

qgd.annotation: White recaptures with the bishop, keeping it on the strong a2-g8 diagonal aimed at e6 and f7.
plans: Prepare e4 to seize the center
Maintain pressure on the a2-g8 diagonal
alternatives: The bishop had to recapture -- no real alternative here

qgd.annotation: Black plants the knight on the best square on the board. The knight on d5 attacks the Bg5, which is pinning Be7, and occupies a dominating central outpost supported by c6 and e6.
plans: Force a favorable resolution of the Bg5 pin
Use the d5 knight as a long-term outpost
alternatives: a5 -- preventing b4, but Nd5 is much more active and forcing

qga.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, claiming central space and preparing the Queen's Gambit. The d4 pawn controls c5 and e5, and it's already defended by the queen.
plans: Play c4 next to challenge Black's center
Build a broad pawn center
alternatives: e4 -- leads to more open, tactical games

qga.annotation: Black meets the center head-on. By playing ...d5, Black ensures equal central space and prevents White from dominating with e4 immediately.
plans: Hold the center, develop pieces naturally
alternatives: Nf6 -- the Indian approach, more flexible

qga.annotation: The Queen's Gambit. White offers the c-pawn to pull Black's d5 pawn out of the center. If Black takes, White gets a big center with d4 and eventually e4.
qga.pawnStructure: Central tension: White d4+c4 vs Black d5
plans: If ...dxc4, recapture the pawn or play e4 with a huge center
If ...e6, maintain pressure on d5

qga.annotation: Black accepts the gambit! This is a perfectly valid choice -- Black gives up the center but gains a tempo because White has to spend time winning the pawn back.
