evans-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop actively to c5, creating the Giuoco Piano position ('quiet game' -- though it's about to get very loud). The bishop on c5 mirrors White's pressure by eyeing f2 and controlling the a7-g1 diagonal. This is the position where Captain Evans, playing aboard a mail ship in 1827, invented the gambit we're about to play.
plans: Counter-pressure on f2 and the dark squares
Prepare d6 and solid development
alternatives: Nf6 - the Two Knights Defence, also sharp
Be7 - the Hungarian, more passive

evans-gambit.annotation: The Evans Gambit! We sacrifice a whole pawn on b4 to lure Black's bishop away from the c5 square. Why? Because once that bishop moves, we're going to play c3 and d4, blasting open the center with a massive lead in development. This is one of the most principled gambits in chess -- we're not gambling, we're investing. A pawn for time, space, and a devastating initiative. Morphy, Kasparov, and Nakamura have all crushed opponents with this move.
evans-gambit.pawnStructure: White sacrifices the b-pawn to accelerate central play with c3 and d4.
plans: After Bxb4, play c3 to kick the bishop and prepare d4
Open the center while ahead in development

evans-gambit.annotation: Black takes the pawn -- declining the Evans Gambit with moves like Bb6 is possible but generally gives White a comfortable position for free. By accepting, Black gets a material advantage but falls behind in development. The bishop on b4 is awkwardly placed and will be kicked around, giving White exactly the tempi needed to build a monster center.
alternatives: Bb6 - the Evans Gambit Declined, solid but passive
Ba5 - keeps the bishop active but White still gets c3-d4

evans-gambit.annotation: Immediately kicking the bishop and preparing d4. This is the whole point of the Evans Gambit -- c3 attacks the bishop with tempo and sets up the d4 pawn push. After the bishop retreats, we'll play d4 with a huge center and rapid development. White's compensation for the pawn is already visible: we're about to have pawns on e4 and d4 while Black is scrambling to develop. The initiative is worth more than a pawn here.
plans: d4 next move to blow open the center
Develop all pieces rapidly while Black deals with the bishop
alternatives: O-O - castling first is a different gambit approach but c3 is sharper

evans-gambit.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining the pin on the c3 pawn (it's pinned to the king along the a5-e1 diagonal). This is considered the main line and the best retreat. The bishop stays active on the a5-e1 diagonal and keeps some pressure on White's queenside. Compared to Be7 or Bc5, Ba5 is the most combative choice -- Black isn't giving up the initiative without a fight.
plans: Maintain the pin on c3 to slow White's d4 plans
Develop quickly and try to consolidate the extra pawn
alternatives: Be7 - safe retreat but passive, gives White easy d4
Bc5 - returns to the original square but blocks c7 pawn

evans-gambit.annotation: The center explodes! d4 is the culmination of the Evans Gambit -- we now have a massive pawn center on e4 and d4, attacking Black's e5 pawn. If Black takes on d4, we recapture with the bishop or open even more lines. The position is opening up and our pieces are going to pour into the center. This is what we sacrificed the pawn for -- look at all those open diagonals and the lead in development.
evans-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e4-d4 center is dominant, attacking Black's e5 and opening lines for all pieces.
plans: Open the center and exploit the development advantage
Castle and bring the full army into the attack

evans-gambit.annotation: Black takes on d4, which opens the center -- exactly what White wants in a gambit. But Black doesn't have a great alternative: if Black doesn't take, White plays dxe5 winning a pawn back with an even better position. So Black accepts that the center opens and tries to weather the storm. The challenge now is that White has open lines and rapid development, while Black still needs several moves to get organized.
alternatives: d6 - tries to hold the center but dxe5 dxe5, Qxd8+ is unpleasant

evans-gambit.annotation: We castle into the attack! Rather than recapturing on d4 immediately, we get the king safe and bring the rook into play. The f1 rook will soon land on d1 or support the center. This is strong psychology too -- we're so confident in our position that we don't even bother taking the pawn back immediately. The threat of cxd4 is always there, and meanwhile we're fully developed while Black is still struggling.
plans: cxd4 when the time is right to establish a massive center
Develop with Ba3, Nbd2, or Qb3 to increase pressure
alternatives: cxd4 - recapturing immediately is also strong but O-O is more flexible

evans-gambit.annotation: Black plays d6 to shore up the e5 square (which doesn't have a pawn anymore) and open a retreat for the bishop to b6. d6 is a solid, defensive move that tries to stabilize the position. Black's plan is simple: survive the initial onslaught, keep the extra pawn, and reach an endgame where material matters more than initiative. But White's lead in development means the middlegame comes first.
evans-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the center but creates a relatively passive structure.
plans: Stabilize and develop the remaining pieces
Keep the extra pawn and trade down to an endgame
alternatives: Nf6 - develops and attacks e4 but d6 is needed for the bishop retreat

evans-gambit.annotation: Now we recapture, establishing the ideal pawn center: e4 and d4. This is a textbook dream center -- two pawns abreast controlling c5, d5, e5, and f5. Combined with our castled king and developed pieces, this center gives us enormous spatial advantage. Every piece has open lines, the bishop on c4 is a monster, and the d4 pawn controls key squares. The Evans Gambit has delivered exactly what it promised.
evans-gambit.pawnStructure: The e4-d4 center is the pinnacle of the Evans Gambit -- total central domination for the cost of one pawn.
plans: Develop remaining pieces and build a kingside attack
Use the center pawns to restrict Black's pieces

evans-gambit.annotation: The bishop retreats to b6, where it's solid but no longer putting pressure on the c3-e1 diagonal. From b6 the bishop eyes d4 and f2, which provides some counterplay, but it's mostly a defensive retreat. Black is trying to complete development and get the king castled. The bishop on b6 is decent but compared to White's actively placed pieces, it's clear who has the initiative.
plans: Pressure d4 and the f2 point
Support d5 break in the future
alternatives: Be7 - very passive but solid
Bc7 - similar idea to b6 but more cramped

evans-gambit.annotation: Knight to c3, developing the last minor piece and adding more firepower to the center. The knight controls d5 -- a dream outpost -- and supports the e4 pawn. From c3 it can jump to d5 at the perfect moment for a devastating blow. We now have every piece developed or near-developed (just the c1 bishop and rooks to activate), while Black is still struggling to castle. This development lead is the entire compensation for the Evans Gambit pawn.
plans: Nd5 at the right moment for a crushing blow
Bg5 or Ba3 to complete development and increase pressure
alternatives: Bg5 - pins the knight immediately but Nc3 is more flexible

evans-gambit.annotation: Black finally develops the kingside knight to f6, attacking e4 and preparing to castle. This is long overdue -- Black needed this knight in the game several moves ago. Nf6 is the most natural and necessary move, defending against White's central dominance and getting one step closer to castling. But White still has a significant development advantage and the powerful center. The Evans Gambit middlegame is just beginning, and White's initiative is very real.
plans: Castle kingside urgently
Challenge e4 with the Nf6 and look for d5 counterplay
alternatives: Na5 - harasses the Bc4 but leaves the kingside undeveloped

stafford-gambit.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn — the most popular first move in chess. Little does White know, they may be walking into one of the trickiest gambits in modern online chess. The Stafford Gambit, popularized by Eric Rosen, is lurking just a few moves away.
plans: Control the center and develop rapidly
Head toward an Open Game
alternatives: d4 — Queen's Pawn, avoiding Stafford territory
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Symmetric e4/e5 pawn center typical of Open Games. Both sides have equal central control.

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black mirrors with e5, entering the Open Game. This symmetrical setup is the gateway to the Stafford Gambit. Black is already planning to meet Nf3 with Nf6, steering toward the Petrov's Defense structure where the Stafford surprise lives.
plans: Play Nf6 after Nf3 to enter Stafford territory
Keep the position open for tactical tricks

stafford-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to its natural square, attacking the e5 pawn and preparing to castle. This is the most common second move. Black's response here separates the Stafford Gambit from the normal Petrov's Defense.
plans: Attack e5 and gain central control
Develop naturally with Bc4 or Bb5

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black plays Nf6, entering the Petrov's Defense. But Black has no intention of playing a quiet Petrov! This knight attacks e4, and when White captures on e5, Black's devastating reply Nc6 will turn the tables.
plans: After Nxe5, play Nc6 — the Stafford Gambit move
Sacrifice the e5 pawn for rapid development and tricks
alternatives: Nc6 — normal development, defending e5

stafford-gambit.annotation: White grabs the free pawn — this looks great, and in normal Petrov's play Black would respond with d6 or Nxe4. But the Stafford Gambit diverges here. White's knight on e5 is seemingly dominant, but it's actually a target.
plans: Consolidate the extra pawn
Maintain the strong knight on e5
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White captures the e5 pawn, gaining a material advantage. The center is now asymmetric with only White having a central pawn.

stafford-gambit.annotation: THIS is the Stafford Gambit! Instead of the normal d6 or Nxe4, Black plays Nc6, immediately attacking White's knight on e5. Eric Rosen has made this move famous — it's objectively dubious, but absolutely LOADED with traps. Black sacrifices a pawn for rapid piece development, open lines, and a web of tactical tricks.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has sacrificed the e5 pawn for nothing material, but the knight on c6 attacks e5 while developing. If White plays Nxc6, Black gets dxc6 with open lines and the bishop pair.
plans: After Nxc6 dxc6, get open d-file and bishop pair
Set traps involving Bc5, Ng4, Qd4 ideas
Play for rapid development and king-hunting
alternatives: d6 — normal Petrov's, safe but boring
Nxe4 — Petrov's main line, solid equality

stafford-gambit.annotation: White captures the knight — the most natural response. White is now up a clean pawn. But this is exactly what Black wanted! The d-file is about to blow open, and Black's pieces will pour out with terrifying speed.
plans: Simplify and hold the extra pawn
Develop calmly with d3, Be2, O-O
alternatives: Nf3 — retreating, but Black gets Nxe4 with tempo

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the d-pawn, and suddenly the position is ALIVE. The d-file is wide open, the queen can swing to d4 or d6, the c8 bishop is free, and Black's pieces coordinate beautifully. White is up a pawn, but Black has open lines, the bishop pair, and tactical ideas everywhere. Welcome to trap city!
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has doubled c-pawns but a wide-open d-file and free piece play. The pawn structure favors dynamic play, which is exactly what Black wants.
plans: Develop Bc5 to target f2
Play Ng4 to create threats on f2 and h2
Use the open d-file for queen and rook pressure

stafford-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to c3 — natural and common. White wants to control d5 and develop normally. But this allows Black's most famous trap: the 'Oh No My Queen' line. By not playing d3 first, White leaves f2 dangerously weak.
plans: Control d5 and develop the bishop
Castle kingside and consolidate
alternatives: d3 — the most careful move, but still tricky
Be2 — develops but remains passive
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White has pawns on e4 with a knight on c3 controlling d5. Black has doubled c-pawns but the open d-file compensates with dynamic chances.

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to the most aggressive diagonal, aiming straight at f2. This is the start of the 'Oh No My Queen' trap. The bishop on c5 creates devastating threats along the a7-g1 diagonal, and f2 is only defended by the king.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: The open center means pieces are everything. Black's bishop on c5 is a monster, bearing down on f2 which is only guarded by the king.
plans: Follow up with Ng4, attacking f2 with two pieces
Bring the queen to h4 for the 'Oh No My Queen' setup
Maintain pressure on f2 and keep White off balance

stafford-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2 — passive but seemingly safe. White is preparing to castle and thinks the position is fine. But this walks into the full 'Oh No My Queen' trap. Black now has a devastating sequence starting with Ng4.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Consolidate the extra pawn
alternatives: h3 — prevents Ng4 but wastes time
d3 — more solid but still allows tricks

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black brings the knight to g4, attacking f2 with TWO pieces (Bc5 and Ng4). This is the key attacking move. White is under immediate pressure — f2 is attacked twice and only defended by the king. The knight also eyes h2 and e3.
plans: Threaten Bxf2+ with devastating discovered attacks
If White castles, attack h2 with the knight
Bring the queen to h4 for the combination

stafford-gambit.annotation: White castles, trying to get the king to safety. This seems logical but is actually the WORST move, walking straight into the full Stafford trap. After castling, the king is on g1, right in the line of fire from Bc5. Black's next move Qh4 will be absolutely crushing.
plans: Get the king to safety
Develop remaining pieces
alternatives: Bxg4 — best defense, giving back material
d3 — tries to hold but Qh4 is still strong
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White has a solid e4-f2-g2-h2 pawn chain but f2 is the critical weakness, attacked by both the Bc5 and Ng4.

stafford-gambit.annotation: The queen swings to h4 with devastating effect! Black now threatens Qxf2# checkmate as well as Qxh2#. The classic 'Oh No My Queen' trap is fully loaded — if White plays h3 trying to kick the knight, Black has the brilliant Nxf2 sacrifice.
plans: Threaten Qxf2# — immediate checkmate
If h3, play Nxf2 with Rxf2 Qxf2+ winning
Maintain maximum pressure on f2, g2, and h2

stafford-gambit.annotation: White plays h3, trying to kick the knight away. This looks like it solves the problem, but this is exactly what Black wanted! After Nxf2, the position explodes in Black's favor.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White has weakened the kingside with h3, creating targets and opening lines for Black's attack.
plans: Kick the knight and stabilize
Hope to consolidate
alternatives: Bxg4 — best defense, but Qxg4 keeps threats
d4 — tries to block the bishop

stafford-gambit.annotation: The knight sacrifices itself on f2! This is the signature move. The knight forks the rook on f1 and opens the diagonal. If Rxf2, Black plays Qxf2+ and the king is forced to the corner.
plans: After Rxf2, play Qxf2+ winning the exchange minimum
The attack continues with Bxh3 ideas
Black has a winning attack in all variations

stafford-gambit.annotation: White captures the knight with the rook — the most natural recapture. But now Black's queen swoops in with a devastating check.
plans: Try to hold with the extra exchange
Defend the kingside
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: After the Nxf2 sacrifice, White's kingside pawn structure is shattered. The f-file is open and the h3 pawn is isolated.

stafford-gambit.annotation: The queen captures on f2 with check! On Kh2, Black has Qg3+ followed by Qg2#. So the king must go to h1. The 'Oh No My Queen' combination is reaching its climax.
plans: After Kh1, play Qxe2 winning the bishop
After Kh2, play Qg3+ and Qg2# checkmate
The attack is decisive in all lines

stafford-gambit.annotation: The king retreats to h1, avoiding immediate checkmate. On Kh2, Black has Qg3+ and Qg2#. The king hides in the corner, but Black's attack continues.
plans: Survive in the corner
Hope to untangle

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the bishop on e2, winning a full piece while maintaining the attack. The queen on e2 eyes g2 and the weak light squares around White's king. Black is now up significant material.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has won back more than the sacrificed pawn. White's king is permanently exposed on h1 with a weakened pawn shield.
plans: Consolidate the material advantage
Attack the weak light squares
Bring rooks into play via the open d-file

stafford-gambit.annotation: White tries to block the bishop diagonal and open a line for the c1 bishop. But it's too little too late — Black is up a piece with a continuing attack, and Bxh3 is coming to rip open the kingside.
plans: Open the c1 bishop diagonal
Create breathing room

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the h3 pawn with the bishop, ripping open the kingside. This threatens Bxg2+ followed by devastating discoveries. If White takes with gxh3, Black has Qf3+ forcing Kg1 and then the queen infiltrates decisively.
plans: Threaten Bxg2+ with discovered attacks
After gxh3, play Qf3+ with a winning attack
Demolish White's pawn cover around the king
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: After d3 Bxh3, White's kingside is completely demolished. The g and h pawns are gone, leaving the king exposed.

stafford-gambit.annotation: White plays Qf1, trying to guard f2 and g2 while keeping the queen active. This is one of White's better defensive tries, but the position is already beyond saving. Black has a bishop on h3 hammering at g2, a queen on e2, and a bishop on c5 controlling the dark squares.
plans: Defend g2 with the queen
Try to consolidate and trade pieces
alternatives: gxh3 — opens the king further, Qf3+ is crushing

stafford-gambit.annotation: The bishop captures on g2 with check, blowing open the last barrier around White's king! The king on h1 must deal with this check. After Kh2, Black recaptures the queen with Bxf1 and is up massive material. The Stafford Gambit has torn White's position to shreds.
plans: Force the king to h2, then take the queen
Win decisive material with the bishop captures
The attack has completely destroyed White's position

stafford-gambit.annotation: The king steps to h2, the only square. After Kxg2, Black has Qxf1 winning the queen. So Kh2 keeps the queen on f1, but Black simply takes it anyway.
plans: Avoid Kxg2 Qxf1
Try to survive with the queen

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the queen! The bishop on g2 discovered an attack, and now Black takes the queen on f1 for free. Black is now up a queen for a bishop — the material advantage is overwhelming. The Stafford Gambit trap has delivered a devastating blow.
plans: Consolidate with the extra queen
Bring the rooks into play
Convert the winning position

stafford-gambit.annotation: White captures the queen with the knight, getting some material back. The position is still completely lost — Black has two bishops and will soon activate the rooks. But White fights on, hoping for some miracle in the complications.
plans: Win back some material
Try to activate the remaining pieces
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has the bishop pair against a knight, and the open position with no pawns on the kingside strongly favors the bishops.

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures, and now has two bishops against a knight and bishop. More importantly, White's king is completely exposed on h2 with no pawn cover. Black will castle and bring the rooks into the attack to finish the game.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White has a shattered kingside — the h and g pawns are gone. Black's doubled c-pawns are irrelevant when the attack is this strong.
plans: Castle and activate the rooks
Use the bishop pair in the open position
Target the exposed white king

stafford-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to g5, trying to create some activity. The bishop on g5 threatens to pin a future knight on f6 and controls some dark squares. But with no queen and an exposed king, White's position is hopeless.
plans: Activate the bishop
Create some threats on the dark squares

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, bringing the king to safety and connecting the rooks. Now both rooks are ready to enter the attack via the open d-file or f-file. Black's development is complete while White is still struggling to coordinate. The material and positional advantages are decisive.
plans: Activate the rooks on the open files
Target White's weak pawns and exposed king
Convert the winning advantage

stafford-gambit.annotation: White brings the rook to e1, trying to centralize and create some counterplay against the e2 bishop. With the queen gone, White's only hope is to trade pieces and reach an endgame, but Black's material advantage and active pieces make this impossible.
plans: Attack the e2 bishop
Try to trade pieces and simplify

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the d3 pawn, winning yet another pawn while keeping the bishop active. The bishop on d3 is perfectly placed — it controls the c4-f1 diagonal and supports future rook infiltrations. Black is now up a full piece plus pawns with a completely winning position.
plans: Win more material and simplify
Prepare rook infiltration on the d-file
The game is effectively over

stafford-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the c-pawn, but now the c-file is open too. Black's position is overwhelming — open files everywhere, the bishop pair dominating, and White's pieces are passively placed. The rook on e1 is the only active piece White has.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White's pawn structure is now a3/b2/d3/e4 — isolated and weak. Black will pick these off one by one while maintaining the attack.
plans: Recapture and try to hold
Use the d-pawn as a passed pawn hope

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black brings the rook to the open d-file, targeting the weak d3 pawn. The rook on d8 is perfectly placed — it x-rays through to the white king's position and will win the d3 pawn easily. Black's complete domination of the open files is the final nail in the coffin.
plans: Win the d3 pawn with the rook
Double rooks on the d-file
Finish the game with overwhelming material

stafford-gambit.annotation: White repositions the bishop to f4, trying to control some central squares and prevent Black's pieces from infiltrating too easily. But with such a large material deficit, this is merely delaying the inevitable.
plans: Control the d6 square
Try to create some fortress setup
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White's remaining pawns on b2 and a2 are weak targets. Black's rook on the third rank and bishop pair dominate the open position.

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the d3 pawn with the rook, now winning another pawn. The rook on d3 is incredibly active — it controls the entire third rank and can swing to the kingside to deliver threats against White's king. Black is completely winning.
plans: Use the active rook to create threats
Win more pawns and convert
The position is resignable for White

stafford-gambit.annotation: White brings the king toward the center, trying to be more active. With so little material remaining, the king needs to participate. But Black's rook on d3 and bishop pair completely dominate the position.
plans: Centralize the king for the endgame
Try to hold with active defense

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black plays Rd2+ — the rook invades to the second rank with check, forcing the white king to move. The rook on the seventh (second) rank is a classic winning pattern, cutting the king off and threatening to gobble up pawns.
plans: Invade the second rank with the rook
Attack the b2 and a2 pawns
Force the king to an inferior square
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: The king is forced to march forward due to the lack of pawn shelter. White's isolated pawns on b2 and a2 are easy targets.

stafford-gambit.annotation: The king steps to f3, getting out of the check. The king on f3 is exposed but there aren't many alternatives. Black will now consolidate the bishop on d4 where it controls the center and supports the rook.
plans: Centralize and try to hold
Avoid further checks

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black centralizes the bishop on d4 — an absolute powerhouse square. The bishop controls the a7-g1 diagonal AND the h8-a1 diagonal, dominating the center. It also supports the rook on d2 and prevents White's rook from accessing key squares. Combined with the other bishop and rook, Black's pieces form a perfect attacking formation.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's remaining pawns are healthy while White's are isolated and falling. The bishop pair controls all the key diagonals in this open position.
plans: Dominate the center with the centralized bishop
Support the rook invasion on the second rank
Win the remaining white pawns

stafford-gambit.annotation: White retreats the bishop to g3, trying to hold onto some dark-square control. The bishop on g3 is passive but at least defends the h2 square and keeps an eye on the e1 rook's retreat. The position is still completely lost for White.
plans: Maintain the dark-squared bishop
Try to create a blockade

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black gobbles up the b2 pawn, collecting another pawn while maintaining the rook on the second rank. White's queenside pawns are falling like dominoes. The a2 pawn will be next, and then Black will have a massive passed a-pawn to support the winning attack.
plans: Win the a2 pawn next
Create passed pawns on the queenside
Complete domination of the position

stafford-gambit.annotation: White advances the king to f4, trying to be active and support the e4 pawn. The king on f4 looks aggressive but is actually vulnerable — Black's bishops and rook can create discovered checks and tactical threats. White is just trying to delay the inevitable.
plans: Support the e4 pawn
Try to advance the e-pawn
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has won all of White's queenside pawns. The connected c and a pawns for Black create passed pawn potential.

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black captures the last queenside pawn! The a-file is now completely clear, and Black has a huge material advantage — two bishops, a rook, and connected passed pawns on the queenside. White's position is absolutely hopeless.
plans: Push the a-pawn and create a passed pawn
Use the rook and bishops to support promotion
Force resignation

stafford-gambit.annotation: White pushes e5, trying to activate the pawn and create some counterplay. A passed pawn in the center can be dangerous, but with Black's overwhelming material advantage and active pieces, this is just a final gasp.
plans: Push the passed e-pawn
Create threats with the advancing pawn

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black advances the c-pawn, creating a passed pawn duo on the queenside. The c5 and a7 pawns, supported by the bishops and rook, represent an unstoppable force. White cannot deal with threats on both flanks simultaneously.
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White pushes the last central pawn in desperation. Black's connected queenside pawns and active pieces are overwhelming.
plans: Advance the queenside pawns
Use the bishops to support pawn promotion
The position is completely winning

stafford-gambit.annotation: White puts the rook on d1, trying to create some activity on the d-file and potentially attack the bishop on d4. But Black's position is too solid and too strong — the bishop on d4 is untouchable and Black's queenside pawns are marching.
plans: Attack the d4 bishop
Try to blockade the queenside pawns

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black plays Be3+ — a check that forces the king to retreat. The bishop on e3 attacks the king and also supports the c5 pawn's advance. This is a classic technique: using checks to gain tempo while improving piece placement. White's position is collapsing.
plans: Force the king back with check
Improve the bishop position
Continue the queenside pawn advance

stafford-gambit.annotation: The king goes to e4, stepping forward into the center. There are no safe squares — on Kf5, Bd3+ wins the rook; on Kg5, Be7+ is devastating. The king on e4 looks bold but is actually vulnerable to Ra4+ ideas. White is in complete zugzwang territory.
plans: Try to use the king actively
Hope Black makes an error
alternatives: Kf5 — allows Bd3+ winning the rook
Kg5 — allows Be7+ with decisive threats

stafford-gambit.annotation: Black plays Ra4+ — a rook check that forces the king to make yet another concession. The rook on a4 simultaneously checks the king, controls the fourth rank, and supports the c-pawn advance. This is the final illustration of the Stafford Gambit's devastating power — from a simple one-pawn sacrifice, Black has generated a won position with beautiful piece coordination. The 'Oh No My Queen' line has delivered everything it promised.
plans: Force the king to a worse square
Maintain the attack with checks
Convert the winning position into checkmate or promotion
stafford-gambit.pawnStructure: White's king is exposed in the center with no pawn cover. Black's rook and bishops completely dominate.

englund-gambit.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, aiming for a solid, strategic game. Most d4 players expect classical responses like d5, Nf6, or the Indian defenses. They are NOT expecting what Black is about to throw at them. The Englund Gambit is a surprise weapon that catches d4 players completely off guard.
plans: Build a strong center with c4
Develop pieces in the Queen's Gambit or Indian style
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White's d4 pawn controls c5 and e5, the foundation of Queen's Pawn openings.

englund-gambit.annotation: The Englund Gambit! Black immediately challenges d4 with e5, offering a pawn sacrifice on move ONE. This is objectively dubious — engines disapprove — but that's not the point. The Englund is a surprise weapon designed for quick games. Most d4 players have never seen this and will be in unfamiliar territory instantly.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: Black offers the e5 pawn to disrupt White's classical d4 setup. If White takes, Black gets immediate counterplay with Nc6 and Qe7 pressuring the e-file.
plans: Sacrifice the e5 pawn for rapid development
After dxe5, play Nc6 and Qe7 to pressure e5
Set up tactical tricks that d4 players won't know
alternatives: d5 — classical, solid Queen's Gambit territory
Nf6 — Indian Defense, the most popular reply

englund-gambit.annotation: White accepts the gambit — the most critical response. White is now up a pawn, but the e5 pawn is advanced and can become a target. Black will immediately start pressuring this pawn, and White needs to be careful not to waste time defending it.
plans: Hold the extra e5 pawn
Develop while maintaining material advantage
alternatives: e3 — declining, safe but passive

englund-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight and immediately pressures e5. This is the key move — the knight on c6 attacks e5 while preparing to support the queen's entry to e7. Black already has a developed piece while White has none.
plans: Play Qe7 next to add more pressure to e5
Develop pieces rapidly while White figures out defense

englund-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight and defends the e5 pawn — a sensible move. The knight on f3 supports the extra pawn and prepares to castle. But White is playing reactively, which is exactly what Black wants in the Englund.
plans: Consolidate the extra pawn on e5
Develop and castle kingside
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e5 pawn is advanced but becomes a target. The asymmetric pawn structure creates the tension the Englund seeks.

englund-gambit.annotation: The queen enters the battle, pinning the e5 pawn against the king on e1. This is the main line of the Englund Gambit — the queen on e7 puts enormous pressure on e5 and opens the possibility of tactical tricks. The position is full of landmines for White.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e5 pawn is the focal point. Black's pieces are all aimed at it, and if it falls, Black equalizes or gets an edge from active pieces.
plans: Regain the e5 pawn with excellent piece activity
Set up Nb4-Nd3+ fork tricks
Develop the bishops to active squares

englund-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to f4, adding another defender to the e5 pawn. This looks solid — the pawn is now defended by the knight and the bishop. But this move has a critical flaw: it leaves the b4 square unguarded, and Black's queen is about to exploit it with a devastating check.
plans: Reinforce the e5 pawn
Develop and prepare to castle
alternatives: Nc3 — more solid, avoids the Qb4+ trick
Bg5 — pins the potential Nf6 but leaves e5 less defended

englund-gambit.annotation: The devastating Qb4+! Black's queen swoops to b4 with check, forking the white king and the b2 pawn. This is THE key trick in the Englund Gambit. White must block the check, and after Bd2, Black takes the free b2 pawn with the queen, winning material and threatening the a1 rook. Many d4 players resign in shock here.
plans: Fork the king and the b2 pawn
After Bd2, take Qxb2 winning a pawn and threatening Ra1
Maintain initiative with piece development

englund-gambit.annotation: White blocks the check with Bd2, the only reasonable response. The bishop retreats from its active f4 square to the passive d2 square, losing a crucial tempo. Now the b2 pawn is completely undefended, and Black's queen is about to feast on it.
plans: Block the check and save the king
Try to recoup the lost tempo
alternatives: Nc3 — blocks check but loses the knight after Qxb2
Nd2 — blocks but loses development time

englund-gambit.annotation: Black captures the b2 pawn and now threatens the a1 rook! The queen on b2 is incredibly powerful — it attacks a1, controls the second rank, and Black has regained the gambit pawn with interest. White must deal with the rook threat immediately, which means losing more time on development.
plans: Threaten the a1 rook
Maintain the queen's dominant position
Continue developing while White scrambles
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has regained the pawn on b2 and the material is equal. White's pawn structure is weakened with doubled pawns potential.

englund-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, blocking the queen's access to c2 and protecting d1. White needs to develop quickly and castle to avoid more tactical problems. The knight on c3 is well-placed, but White has already lost a pawn and significant time.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White is now even on material but has wasted tempo moving the bishop twice. Black's development lead is significant in the open position.
plans: Develop and castle quickly
Try to kick the queen with Rb1

englund-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to b4, pinning the knight on c3 to the king! This is a key follow-up move. The bishop on b4 creates a nasty pin that restricts White's pieces. If the knight moves, the queen might invade to c2 or c3. Black's development is flowing naturally while White is tied in knots.
plans: Pin the Nc3 and restrict White's development
Support the queen's position on b2
Prepare Nxe5 to win back the gambit pawn

englund-gambit.annotation: White attacks the queen with the rook, trying to chase it away from b2. This is the most natural response — the rook is developed to the b-file and forces the queen to retreat. But Black has a safe square on a3 where the queen maintains pressure.
plans: Chase the queen from b2
Develop the rook to an active file

englund-gambit.annotation: The queen retreats to a3, staying active and maintaining pressure on e3, d3, and the a-file. From a3, the queen supports the bishop on b4 and keeps an eye on the c1-a3 diagonal. Black's pieces are well-coordinated while White is still struggling to untangle.
plans: Maintain queen activity on the a3 square
Support the Bb4 pin
Prepare Nxe5 to regain the pawn

englund-gambit.annotation: White plays the strong Nd5, a centralizing move that attacks the bishop on b4 and controls key squares. The knight on d5 is a monster — it radiates influence across the board. This is White's best practical try to seize the initiative back.
plans: Centralize the knight on the dominant d5 square
Attack the Bb4 and force it to retreat or trade
Try to consolidate and develop
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: After Nd5, the central pawn structure is tense. White's e5 pawn is defended but Nd5 puts enormous pressure on the entire formation.

englund-gambit.annotation: Black captures the e5 pawn with the knight! This is a critical moment — Black regains the sacrificed pawn while opening up the position. The knight on e5 is centralized and powerful. Black is now equal on material with better piece activity and a continuing initiative.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: Material is now level. Black has regained the gambit pawn and has active pieces, while White's bishop is misplaced on d2.
plans: Centralize the knight on e5
Maintain the pin on the remaining pieces
Use the active pieces to create more threats

englund-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the knight from d5. White trades the centralized knight to eliminate Black's active piece. But this opens the position further, which favors Black's better-coordinated pieces. The trade simplifies slightly but Black retains the initiative.
plans: Simplify by trading the active knight
Try to consolidate

englund-gambit.annotation: Black captures the bishop on d2 with check! This is a great in-between move — Black wins the bishop while giving check, gaining a tempo. White must deal with the check before doing anything else. Black is now up a piece temporarily, and the check forces White to recapture unfavorably.
plans: Win the bishop with check, gaining tempo
After Qxd2, Black has an active position with threats
The discovered check idea works perfectly

englund-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the queen. The queen on d2 is centralized but White has lost the bishop pair and significant time. Black will now challenge the knight on e5 and continue with active development. The Englund Gambit has achieved its goal — an unbalanced position with attacking chances.
plans: Recapture and try to use the centralized queen
Support the Ne5 with the queen

englund-gambit.annotation: Black repositions the queen to a5, keeping it active on the queenside. From a5, the queen eyes the e5 knight and the a2 pawn. Black also threatens Qxe5, winning the knight for free if White doesn't protect it. The Englund Gambit continues to generate threats.
plans: Threaten Qxe5 winning the knight
Attack the a2 pawn
Maintain pressure from the a5 outpost

englund-gambit.annotation: White plays the shocking Nxc7+, forking the king and the a8 rook! This is White's most aggressive try — winning material with a knight fork. But Black knew this was coming and has prepared for it. The temporary material loss will be compensated by active piece play.
plans: Fork the king and rook to win the exchange
Try to equalize with the extra material
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: After Nxc7+ and Nxa8, White has won the exchange but the knight on a8 is trapped far from the action. Black's central pawn majority compensates.

englund-gambit.annotation: The king steps to d8, getting out of check. The king on d8 looks awkward but is actually safe for now — it can later go to c8 or e7. Black allows White to take the rook, knowing that the queen on a5 and rapid development will provide enough compensation.
plans: Get out of check and stabilize
After Nxa8, recapture with Qxe5 and develop quickly
Use the active pieces to compensate for the exchange

englund-gambit.annotation: White takes the rook, winning the exchange. White is now up a full exchange (rook for knight), which is normally a significant advantage. But the knight on a8 is trapped in the corner and will take many moves to return to the game. Black will use this time to develop rapidly and create threats.
plans: Win the exchange and try to consolidate
Eventually rescue the Na8

englund-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures the e5 pawn with the queen, now threatening Qxe2# or at least Qb2 with massive activity. The queen on e5 is a beast — it controls the center, targets e2, and can swing to either flank. Meanwhile White's knight on a8 is completely offside. Black's activity compensates for the exchange deficit.
plans: Centralize the queen and dominate the board
Target e2 and the weak light squares
Develop Nf6 and the bishops with tempo

englund-gambit.annotation: White plays e3, blocking the queen's access to e2 and preparing to develop the bishop. This is the most solid defensive move — it creates a barrier and prepares Bd3 and castling. But White is still struggling with the trapped pawn on a8.
plans: Block the e-file threats
Develop Bd3 and castle kingside
Eventually rescue the Na8
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's d5 pawn gives a strong central stake. Combined with the Nf6 and active queen, the center belongs to Black despite the material deficit.

englund-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, an excellent square. The knight controls d5, e4, g4, and h5 — all important squares. Black's development is now flowing smoothly. With the knight on f6 and the queen on e5, Black has a dangerous attacking setup.
plans: Develop naturally with the knight
Prepare Bg4 to pin the Nf3 and create more pressure
Support future kingside attacks

englund-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to d3, a good square that controls e4 and prepares castling. The bishop on d3 is an important defensive piece — it guards the light squares and supports the e3 pawn chain. White is slowly getting organized but the knight on a8 remains a liability.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White has a solid e3-f2-g2-h2 pawn structure but the knight on a8 is effectively out of the game, making it feel like playing a piece down.
plans: Complete development and castle
Use the bishop to control e4 and the kingside diagonals
Try to find a route for the Na8 back into the game

englund-gambit.annotation: Black pushes d5, gaining central space and opening lines for the bishops. The pawn on d5 stakes a claim in the center and prepares to develop the c8 bishop to g4 or f5. Black's position is harmonious — all pieces are working together while White's knight is still stuck on a8.
plans: Gain central space with d5
Open the diagonal for the c8 bishop
Create a strong pawn center

englund-gambit.annotation: White finally castles kingside, getting the king to safety. This was long overdue — the king on e1 was exposed to tactical threats. Now White can think about consolidating, but Black's active pieces and the trapped Na8 still pose significant problems.
plans: Get the king to safety
Connect the rooks and consolidate

englund-gambit.annotation: Black pins the knight on f3 to the queen! The bishop on g4 is perfectly placed — it creates a pin that restricts White's most active piece. If the knight moves, the queen on d2 falls. Black's development is now complete (except for castling, which isn't urgent with the king on d8), and the position is full of tactical energy.
plans: Pin the Nf3 to the queen
Increase pressure on the kingside
Prepare Ne4 to invade with the knight
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White's f3 push weakens the e3 pawn and the king's pawn cover. The g1-a7 diagonal is slightly weakened.

englund-gambit.annotation: White plays Rb5, trying to get the rook active on the fifth rank. The rook attacks the d5 pawn laterally and hopes to swing to the kingside. But this move allows Black to centralize the queen further and increase the pressure.
plans: Activate the rook on the fifth rank
Attack the d5 pawn
Try to swing the rook to the kingside

englund-gambit.annotation: Black repositions the queen to d6, defending the d5 pawn while maintaining central dominance. The queen on d6 controls key squares and supports a potential Ne4 invasion. Black's position is extremely harmonious — the queen, knight, and bishop all work together perfectly.
plans: Defend d5 while staying active
Support Ne4 invasion
Keep maximum pressure on White's position

englund-gambit.annotation: White plays f3, trying to break the pin on the knight and challenge the bishop on g4. This weakens the kingside slightly but it's necessary to free the knight from the pin. After the bishop retreats, White hopes to finally coordinate the pieces.
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: The f3 push weakens the e3 pawn and the g1-a7 diagonal, but it frees the f3 knight from the pin. White is making concessions to unpin.
plans: Break the pin on the Nf3
Chase the bishop from g4
Try to reorganize the pieces

englund-gambit.annotation: The bishop retreats to h5, staying on the a2-g8 diagonal and maintaining tension. From h5, the bishop keeps an eye on the f3 pawn (which is now a target) and can potentially swing to g6 to attack the white king. Black's pieces remain extremely well-coordinated.
plans: Maintain bishop activity on the h5-e2 diagonal
Target the weakened f3 pawn
Support the knight's invasion to e4
englund-gambit.pawnStructure: White's knight returns via c7, but the time spent rescuing it has given Black free development. The position is dynamically balanced.

englund-gambit.annotation: White finally begins the long journey to rescue the trapped knight. Nc7 is the first step — from c7, the knight can go to d5, e6, or a6. It has taken many moves to get this piece back into the game, which illustrates how much time White lost from the Nxa8 decision. Meanwhile Black has had free reign to develop and attack.
plans: Rescue the trapped knight via c7-d5 or c7-e6
Get the knight back into the game
Try to reach a playable middlegame

englund-gambit.annotation: Black invades with Ne4! The knight lands on the powerful e4 square, supported by the d5 pawn. From e4, the knight attacks d2, f2, c3, and g3 — practically every important square. This is the culmination of Black's strategy in the Englund Gambit: rapid development, piece activity, and centralized knights that dominate the board. White's position is under severe pressure despite being up the exchange.
plans: Dominate the center with the Ne4 outpost
Attack d2, f2, and the weak light squares
Coordinate with the queen and bishop for a winning attack

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, the most popular first move in chess. Against the Sicilian Defense, White has a special surprise weapon: the Smith-Morra Gambit. Instead of the mainline Open Sicilian with Nf3 and d4, White will sacrifice a pawn immediately for rapid development and attacking chances.
plans: Prepare the Smith-Morra Gambit with d4 and c3
Sacrifice a pawn for fast development and open lines
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The starting position of the e4 opening. White aims for central control which will transform into the Smith-Morra's IQP structure.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: The Sicilian Defense — Black's most popular and aggressive response to e4. The c5 pawn challenges White's control of d4 and creates an asymmetrical position. Black fights for the center from the flank, and the resulting positions are typically sharp and combative.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's c5 pawn fights for d4 control without committing to a central pawn structure yet. This creates the imbalanced positions that make the Sicilian so popular at all levels.
plans: Challenge White's center from the flank
Create an asymmetrical, fighting position
Aim for counterplay on the queenside

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White strikes in the center immediately with d4! This is the hallmark of the Smith-Morra Gambit. Instead of the normal Nf3 followed by d4 (the Open Sicilian), White pushes d4 right away, offering a pawn sacrifice. The idea is simple but powerful: after cxd4 c3, White gets the c-file and rapid development in exchange for the pawn.
plans: Sacrifice the d4 pawn to open the center
Follow up with c3 to offer the gambit pawn
Develop quickly on the open lines
alternatives: Nf3 — the normal Open Sicilian approach
Nc3 — the Closed Sicilian

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black captures the d4 pawn — the natural response. Declining the gambit gives White a favorable transposition. By taking, Black wins a pawn but opens the center, which is exactly what the Smith-Morra player wants. The critical question is whether Black can hold the extra pawn or White's development advantage will prove decisive.
plans: Win a pawn and try to hold it
Develop carefully to neutralize White's initiative
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: Black captures on d4, creating an open center. The pawn exchange opens lines that White will exploit with rapid development.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: The Smith-Morra Gambit! White offers a second pawn with c3, inviting Black to capture again. This is the defining move of the opening. If Black takes on c3, White recaptures with the knight and gets a dream setup: rapid development, open c-file, active pieces, and an isolated queen pawn (IQP) structure that provides dynamic chances.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: After dxc3 Nxc3, White will have an IQP on e4 with open c and d-files. This structure gives White dynamic piece play and attacking chances in exchange for a slight structural weakness.
plans: After dxc3 Nxc3, develop rapidly with Bc4, Nf3, O-O
Use the open c-file and active pieces to attack
The IQP provides dynamic play and central control
alternatives: Nf3 — transposes to other Sicilian lines

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black accepts the gambit, capturing the second pawn. Black is now up a full pawn, but White's compensation is about to become apparent. The knight recapture on c3 gives White a developed piece, and the open c-file will become a highway for White's rooks. The battle between material and initiative begins.
plans: Win a second pawn and try to consolidate
Develop carefully and return the pawn if necessary
alternatives: Nf6 — the Declined Variation, keeping the extra pawn on d4
d3 — another way to decline, giving back the pawn for solid play

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the knight, completing the gambit. The knight on c3 is beautifully placed — it controls d5, e4, and supports future piece development. White has sacrificed a pawn but now has a significant lead in development, open central files, and natural attacking chances. This is the starting position of the Smith-Morra Gambit proper.
plans: Develop Bc4 to target f7 and control d5
Play Nf3 and O-O for rapid castling
Use the c-file and central development for attacking play
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: After Nxc3, White has an isolated queen pawn (IQP) on e4. This structure gives dynamic piece play and central control in exchange for the structural weakness.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, the most natural square. The knight controls d4, e5, and helps prepare the kingside development. This is the most common response — Black develops normally while keeping the extra pawn.
plans: Control d4 and e5 with the knight
Develop the kingside with e6, Nf6, Be7
Castle and consolidate the extra pawn

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to the aggressive c4 square, targeting the vulnerable f7 pawn and controlling the d5 square. The bishop on c4 is a key piece in the Smith-Morra — it creates threats along the a2-g8 diagonal and works beautifully with the knight on c3 to control the center.
plans: Target f7 and control d5
Develop Nf3 next and castle quickly
Create pressure on the light-square diagonal

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays e6, reinforcing the d5 square and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop. This is the solid approach — the e6 pawn blocks the Bc4 diagonal and creates a strong pawn chain. However, the light-squared bishop is now somewhat restricted behind the pawns.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's e6-d6 pawn chain is solid but slightly passive. The light-squared bishop will need to find a good square, often via d7 or b7 after b6.
plans: Block the Bc4 diagonal with e6
Prepare d6 and Nf6 development
Castle kingside and consolidate
alternatives: d6 — another solid option, keeping e7 open for the bishop

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, completing the development of both minor pieces. The knight controls d4, e5, and g5 — all important squares. White is now ready to castle and begin the middlegame attack. The development advantage is striking: White has three pieces developed to Black's one.
plans: Castle kingside next move
Complete development with maximum efficiency
Prepare Qe2 and Rd1 for central pressure

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays d6, creating a solid pawn center with e6 and d6. This is the standard Sicilian pawn structure. The d6 pawn supports e5 advances and gives the dark-squared bishop room to develop. However, it also creates the d5 square as a potential outpost for White's pieces.
plans: Create a solid pawn center
Prepare Nf6 and Be7 development
Maintain the defensive pawn chain
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's e6-d6 pawn chain is solid but creates a backward pawn on d6. The d-file will be a key battleground.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White castles kingside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. In just 7 moves, White has completed development of both knights, the bishop, and castled. This is the power of the Smith-Morra — the pawn sacrifice buys incredible development speed. Now White can focus entirely on attacking.
plans: Complete king safety and connect rooks
Prepare Qe2 and Rd1 for central pressure
Launch the middlegame attack with full development

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, a natural and solid move. The knight defends d5, attacks e4, and prepares to castle. Black is catching up in development but is still behind White. The knight on f6 is well-placed but can become a target if White plays e5 at the right moment.
plans: Develop the knight to its natural square
Prepare to castle kingside
Defend the center
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's e6-d6-Nf6 setup is the standard Sicilian defensive formation. The pawns control d5 and create a solid but slightly passive structure.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White places the queen on e2, supporting the e4 pawn and preparing to swing to the kingside. The queen on e2 is a flexible placement — it connects the rooks, supports e4, and can later go to e4 or even h5 for attacking purposes. This is a key setup move in the Smith-Morra.
plans: Support e4 and prepare central action
Connect the rooks and prepare Rd1
Keep options open for Qe4 or kingside attacks

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a solid but somewhat passive square. The bishop on e7 prepares to castle and defends the kingside. In many Smith-Morra lines, Black wishes this bishop were on a more active diagonal, but e7 is the safest option.
plans: Prepare to castle kingside
Maintain a solid defensive setup
Keep the position closed to neutralize White's initiative

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White places the rook on the open d-file, targeting the d6 pawn. The rook on d1 is perfectly placed — it pressures d6 and controls the d-file. Combined with the queen on e2 and the pieces already in play, White has a formidable attacking setup. The development advantage is now fully realized.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: White's rook on d1 pressures d6, the key weakness in Black's camp. The IQP on e4 supports pieces but can become a target in the endgame.
plans: Pressure the d6 pawn along the d-file
Double rooks on the d-file with Rac1-Rcd1
Create threats on the d6 weakness

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety. Both kings are now castled, and the middlegame battle begins in earnest. Black has an extra pawn but White has superior piece activity, open files, and a natural attacking setup. The critical question is whether White can break through before Black consolidates.
plans: Get the king to safety
Prepare to activate the remaining pieces
Try to consolidate the extra pawn

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White develops the last minor piece to the active f4 square. The bishop on f4 targets d6, supports e5 pushes, and adds to the pressure on Black's position. White now has every piece developed and actively placed — this is the dream scenario for the Smith-Morra player.
plans: Increase pressure on d6
Support the e5 pawn break
All pieces are now optimally developed
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: White has completed the ideal Smith-Morra setup: pieces developed, rooks on open files, and the IQP on e4 providing dynamic energy.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to d7, a reasonable but passive square. The bishop connects the rooks and prepares potential counterplay with Rc8. However, the bishop on d7 is not very active compared to White's aggressively placed pieces.
plans: Connect the rooks
Prepare Rc8 for counterplay on the c-file
Keep the position solid

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White seizes the open c-file with the rook! Both rooks are now on open central files — Rc1 on the c-file and Rd1 on the d-file. This is the ideal Smith-Morra setup: total control of the open lines, active minor pieces, and a development advantage that translates into real attacking chances. The pressure on Black's position is immense.
plans: Control the c-file with the rook
Double rooks or switch files as needed
Maintain maximum pressure on the center and queenside

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black contests the c-file by placing a rook on c8. This is a good defensive move — the rook on c8 challenges White's control and prepares potential trades. If Black can exchange rooks on the c-file, it reduces White's attacking potential significantly.
plans: Contest the c-file
Prepare to trade rooks and reduce pressure
Activate the remaining pieces

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White retreats the bishop to b3, keeping it on the active a2-g8 diagonal while making room for the knight to jump to d5. The bishop on b3 still targets e6 and f7, and now the Nd5 move becomes a serious threat. This is a typical Smith-Morra maneuver — repositioning pieces to maximize pressure.
plans: Prepare the powerful Nd5 jump
Maintain pressure on the a2-g8 diagonal
Keep all pieces actively coordinated
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The Nd5 outpost is the crown jewel of the IQP structure. The isolated pawn on e4 supports this powerful knight post.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays a6, preventing Nb5 and creating a potential escape square for the queen on a5. This is a useful prophylactic move, but it doesn't address the main threat of Nd5. Sometimes in the Smith-Morra, Black needs to be more proactive rather than playing waiting moves.
plans: Prevent Nb5 jumps
Prepare b5 expansion on the queenside
Create an escape square for the queen

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: The knight jumps to d5! This is one of the most powerful moves in the Smith-Morra arsenal. The knight on d5 is a monster — it attacks e7, c7, f6 (after exd5 exd5), and completely dominates the center. Black must deal with this immediately or face a devastating attack. The d5 square is the crown jewel of the IQP structure.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The Nd5 jump exploits the IQP structure perfectly. The isolated e4 pawn supported the knight's journey to d5, which is the key outpost in this pawn configuration.
plans: Dominate the center from d5
Create threats against e7, c7, and the entire kingside
Force Black into uncomfortable decisions

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black captures the knight, the most principled response. Leaving the pawn on d5 would be intolerable. But taking opens the e-file and gives White's bishop on b3 a clear diagonal to the king. The position now transforms — White gets open lines and piece activity as compensation for the sacrificed pawn.
plans: Eliminate the powerful knight
Accept the structural consequences
Try to hold the resulting position

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the e-pawn, creating a powerful passed pawn on d5. This passed pawn will be a thorn in Black's side throughout the game. The d5 pawn restricts Black's pieces and threatens to advance to d6, forking or disrupting Black's piece coordination. The e-file is now open for White's rook.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: After exd5, White has a passed d-pawn that will become a major weapon. The open e-file also provides attacking chances for the rooks.
plans: Push d6 to create threats
Use the open e-file for rook activity
The passed d-pawn restricts Black's pieces

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays Na5, trying to eliminate the dangerous bishop on b3 by trading knights for bishops. The knight on a5 targets b3, and if the bishop retreats, the knight can reroute to c4. This is a reasonable plan, but the knight is offside on a5 and takes time to execute.
plans: Trade the knight for the Bb3
Reroute the knight via a5-c4
Eliminate White's dangerous light-squared bishop

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White captures the d6 pawn with the bishop, winning another pawn! The bishop takes on d6 and creates a discovered attack threat. White is temporarily sacrificing the bishop pair but gaining a crucial pawn and opening lines. The d5 and d6 pawns together form a powerful passed pawn duo.
plans: Win the d6 pawn and create a passed pawn duo
Open lines with the bishop capture
Increase the pressure on Black's position
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The Na5 knight is heading for b3 to eliminate White's bishop. This exchange will weaken White's light square control but Black's knight is offside.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the bishop, restoring material balance on the d6 square. The bishop on d6 is more active than it was on e7, but Black's position remains cramped due to the d5 pawn. The knight on a5 is still offside, and White's rooks control the open files.
plans: Recapture and try to stabilize
Use the bishop actively on d6
Get the knight back into play

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White exchanges rooks on c8, simplifying the position while maintaining the advantage. By trading rooks, White reduces Black's defensive resources while keeping the powerful d5 pawn and the active pieces. The exchange favors White because it removes a defender from Black's position.
plans: Trade rooks to simplify while ahead
Reduce Black's defensive resources
Maintain the d5 pawn advantage

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the queen. The queen on c8 is not ideally placed — it's somewhat passive and blocks the a8 rook. Black needs to reorganize the pieces, but the d5 pawn keeps everything cramped.
plans: Recapture and try to reorganize
Look for counterplay with the queen
Get the knight back from a5

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White centralizes the queen on the powerful e4 square. From e4, the queen controls the center, targets a8 along the diagonal, eyes h7 for kingside attacks, and supports the d5 pawn. The queen on e4 is a powerhouse — it radiates influence in every direction.
plans: Dominate the center with the queen on e4
Threaten Qxa8 in some lines
Support d5-d6 push and kingside attacks
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: White has doubled b-pawns from axb3 but the open a-file and powerful d5 pawn more than compensate.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black finally captures the bishop, trading the offside knight for White's light-squared bishop. This removes a key attacking piece from White's arsenal. The knight-bishop trade generally favors the side giving up the knight in open positions, but here Black needed to eliminate the dangerous b3 bishop.
plans: Eliminate the dangerous Bb3
Simplify and try to hold the position
Use the extra tempo to reorganize

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the a-pawn. The pawn structure has changed — White now has doubled b-pawns, but the a-file is open for potential rook use. More importantly, White's queen on e4 and rook on d1 still create tremendous central pressure, and the d5 pawn remains a powerful asset.
plans: Use the open a-file if needed
Maintain central pressure with Qe4 and Rd1
Push d6 at the right moment

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays g6, giving the king a luft (breathing room) on g7 and preventing back-rank tricks. This also supports Bf5, which would challenge White's dominant queen on e4. The move is solid but slightly weakens the dark squares around the king.
plans: Create king safety with g6 luft
Prepare Bf5 to challenge the Qe4
Solidify the kingside

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White brings the knight to g5, an aggressive jump that threatens Nxf7 (forking the rook and queen) and Nxh7. The knight on g5 creates immediate tactical threats. Combined with the queen on e4, White has a dangerous attacking battery aimed at the kingside.
plans: Threaten Nxf7 fork or Nxh7
Create kingside attacking pressure
Coordinate with the queen for tactical strikes
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The Ng5 threatens to exploit Black's weakened kingside. The g6-h6 pawn formation is vulnerable to knight infiltrations.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to f5, challenging the queen on e4 and defending against the Ng5 threats. The bishop on f5 is an active piece that blocks the f-file and contests the light squares. This is Black's best defensive try — forcing the queen to relocate.
plans: Challenge the queen on e4
Block the f-file and defend f7
Activate the light-squared bishop

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White retreats the queen to f3, maintaining pressure along the f-file and keeping the knight on g5 supported. The queen on f3 still eyes f7 and creates potential threats. White's pieces remain well-coordinated despite the queen retreat.
plans: Maintain pressure on f7
Keep the Ng5 supported
Prepare tactical strikes on the kingside

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black kicks the knight with h6. This is a committal move — it weakens the g6 pawn and the dark squares, but the pawn on g5 was too threatening to leave there. Black must make this concession to avoid immediate tactical problems.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The h6 pawn weakens the g6 pawn (now lacking h7 support) and the dark squares around the king. But it was necessary to challenge the Ng5.
plans: Kick the Ng5 away from the threatening square
Relieve the immediate tactical pressure
Accept the slight kingside weakening

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White retreats the knight to e4, centralizing it on a powerful square. The knight on e4 attacks d6 and f6, and can jump to various aggressive squares. The knight retreat is actually an improvement — from e4, the knight coordinates with the queen on f3 and the d5 pawn to create a dominant central presence.
plans: Centralize the knight on e4
Threaten Nxd6 or Nf6+
Maintain the strong d5 pawn
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: After the Nxe4 trade, fewer pieces favor the side with the passed pawn. White's d5 pawn becomes even more dangerous.

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black captures the knight, eliminating a dangerous piece. The trade simplifies the position slightly, which generally favors the defender in gambit situations. By removing the knight, Black reduces the number of attacking pieces White has.
plans: Simplify by trading knights
Reduce White's attacking potential
Head toward a more manageable endgame

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the queen, returning to the powerful e4 square. The queen on e4 is again a dominant central piece — it eyes both a8 and h7, supports the d5 pawn, and creates constant tactical threats. The simplified position actually helps White because the d5 pawn becomes even more dangerous with fewer pieces to stop it.
plans: Recentralize the queen on the powerful e4 square
Prepare to advance d5-d6
Maintain pressure on the kingside

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black centralizes the bishop on e5, a powerful square that controls key diagonals and blocks the e-file. The bishop on e5 is Black's best piece — it dominates the center and creates defensive fortification. However, the d5 pawn still looms as a major threat.
plans: Centralize the bishop on e5
Block the e-file and control dark squares
Create a defensive stronghold

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White pushes the d-pawn to d6! This is the critical moment — the passed pawn advances deep into Black's territory, creating enormous pressure. The d6 pawn is a thorn that restricts Black's pieces and threatens to promote. Black must constantly watch this pawn while also defending against White's queen and rook.
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The d6 pawn is a monster — deep in enemy territory, it cramps all of Black's pieces and threatens eventual promotion.
plans: Push the passed pawn to d6
Create a permanent weakness in Black's position
Threaten d7 and eventual promotion

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black plays Qc1, a desperate but resourceful move. The queen on c1 creates back-rank threats against White's king and tries to generate counterplay. This is Black's only chance — rather than passively defending the d6 pawn, Black goes for active counterplay.
plans: Create back-rank threats
Generate counterplay against White's king
Force White to deal with defensive issues

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White retreats the queen to d3, defending the d1 rook and maintaining control. The queen on d3 supports the d6 pawn and keeps everything protected. White's position remains dominant despite Black's counterplay attempt.
plans: Defend the rook and maintain control
Support the d6 pawn from d3
Prepare to push d7 at the right moment

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black attacks the rook on d1 with the bishop. This tactical trick forces White to address the bishop threat. Black is fighting hard to generate counterplay, and the bishop on g4 creates an uncomfortable pin. The game has become very tactical.
plans: Attack the rook on d1
Create tactical complications
Force White to make concessions

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: White calmly moves the rook to e1, getting out of the bishop's attack while placing the rook on the open e-file. The rook on e1 targets the Be5 and supports potential back-rank ideas. White's position remains strategically superior with the d6 pawn dominating the board.
plans: Escape the bishop attack
Place the rook on the open e-file
Target the Be5 and maintain pressure

smith-morra-gambit.annotation: Black shifts the bishop to d4, blocking the d-file and keeping the bishop active. The bishop on d4 controls the center and supports the fight against the d6 pawn. Black is making the best of a difficult position, using the bishop to create a blockade on the d-pawn's path to promotion. This concludes a typical Smith-Morra middlegame — White's gambit pawn has transformed into a powerful passed pawn, and the piece activity generated by the early sacrifice has resulted in a lasting positional advantage. The Smith-Morra Gambit proves that a single pawn sacrifice can yield tremendous attacking potential when backed by rapid development and central control.
plans: Blockade the d-pawn with the bishop
Maintain central control
Fight for survival in a difficult endgame
smith-morra-gambit.pawnStructure: The bishop blockade on d4 is Black's only hope against the passed d6 pawn. The endgame battle revolves around this pawn.

scotch-gambit.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, controlling the center and setting the stage for the Scotch Gambit. This is the starting point for one of the most dynamic openings at the club level — a system that combines the open lines of the Scotch with the attacking ideas of the Italian Game.
plans: Control the center and open lines for pieces
Head toward the Scotch Gambit with d4
alternatives: d4 — Queen's Pawn, different character

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black responds classically, occupying the center. This symmetrical structure leads to the Open Game, where the Scotch Gambit lives. Black's e5 pawn is about to become the target of White's d4 advance.
plans: Hold the center and develop pieces
Prepare to meet White's central challenge

scotch-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to its best square, attacking e5 and preparing kingside castling. This sets up the key decision on move 3: the Scotch Gambit with d4. The knight on f3 will be perfectly placed to support the central advance.
plans: Play d4 next for the Scotch Gambit
Develop rapidly toward the kingside

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to defend e5 — the most popular reply. The knight on c6 is natural and solid, but it blocks the c-pawn, which can be a long-term issue. This is the exact position White needs to launch the Scotch Gambit.
plans: Defend the e5 pawn and develop pieces
Prepare d6 or Bc5 for further development
alternatives: Nf6 — the Petrov's Defense, a different approach

scotch-gambit.annotation: White strikes the center with d4! This opens the position and challenges Black to make a critical decision. In the regular Scotch Game, White would recapture with Nxd4 after exd4. But the Scotch GAMBIT diverges — White will play Bc4 instead, sacrificing the d4 pawn for rapid development and initiative.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: The center is about to blow open. After exd4, White will have a pawn on e4 vs nothing in the center for Black, but Black gets an extra d4 pawn that's hard to hold.
plans: After exd4, play Bc4 instead of Nxd4
Sacrifice the d-pawn for a huge development lead
Get the bishop to c4, targeting f7

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black captures the d4 pawn, the most natural response. Now comes the critical moment: will White play Nxd4 for the regular Scotch, or Bc4 for the Scotch Gambit? The gambit move Bc4 leads to much sharper, more exciting play.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Black has an extra pawn on d4, but it's hard to hold. White's e4 pawn controls key central squares and the position is about to open up dramatically.
plans: Hold the extra pawn if possible
Develop quickly to consolidate

scotch-gambit.annotation: The Scotch Gambit! Instead of recapturing with Nxd4, White develops the bishop to its most aggressive diagonal, targeting f7. White gambits the d4 pawn for time and piece activity. The bishop on c4 is a monster — it aims at the weakest point in Black's camp and supports future tactics.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: White is down a pawn but has superior development and the bishop on c4 creating immediate pressure. The open center favors the side with better development — that's White.
plans: Develop with tempo and pressure f7
Castle kingside and bring the rook into the game
Consider c3 to attack the d4 pawn and build a center
alternatives: Nxd4 — the regular Scotch Game, solid but less dynamic

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight and counterattacks the e4 pawn — a logical and strong reply. The knight on f6 creates a real threat to White's center. Black is trying to develop while keeping the extra pawn.
plans: Attack e4 and develop pieces
Look for d5 to return the pawn and equalize
alternatives: Bc5 — also strong, leading to the Giuoco Piano Gambit structure
d6 — solid but passive, giving White free development

scotch-gambit.annotation: White pushes the e-pawn forward with tempo, kicking the knight and seizing more space! This is the aggressive continuation that defines the Scotch Gambit. The e5 pawn is a spearhead in Black's territory, cramping development and controlling key squares like d6 and f6.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: White's e5 pawn is an advanced wedge that restricts Black's pieces, especially the knight. Combined with the bishop on c4, White has a powerful attacking formation despite being down a pawn.
plans: Chase the knight and gain more tempo
Follow up with O-O and Re1 to support e5
Target f7 with combined bishop and pawn pressure

scotch-gambit.annotation: The strongest reply! Black counterstrikes in the center with d5, giving back the pawn but fighting for the initiative. After exf6, Black captures the bishop on c4 and has active piece play. This is the main line — Black returns material to free the position.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Black breaks in the center with d5, challenging White's e5 pawn chain. The position is about to simplify slightly, but the tension remains high.
plans: Trade pawns in the center and activate pieces
After exf6, recapture the bishop on c4
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle
alternatives: Ne4 — keeping the extra pawn but allowing White more attacking chances
Ng4 — aggressive but risky, aiming at e5 and f2

scotch-gambit.annotation: White captures the knight with the e-pawn. Now Black must recapture — Qxf6 is the common choice since it keeps the pawn structure intact and puts the queen on an active square. White has returned the gambit pawn but has initiative with open lines everywhere.
plans: Recapture on d4 with the knight next
Develop quickly: O-O, Re1, Bg5 or Nc3
Exploit the open center and half-open files

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black grabs the bishop on c4, winning a piece for a pawn. Material is roughly equal now. But the position is wide open, and White's remaining pieces are very actively placed. The real battle is activity vs material.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: The center is completely open. Black has pawns on c4 and d4 which are both vulnerable. White needs to target these weak pawns while developing rapidly.
plans: Hold the extra material and try to consolidate
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle
Guard the d4 and c4 pawns

scotch-gambit.annotation: White pushes the pawn to g7, forcing Black to deal with this advanced passed pawn. The g7 pawn attacks the rook on h8 and prevents Black from castling kingside normally. Black must recapture, weakening the kingside pawn structure.
plans: Force Black to recapture and weaken the kingside
Use the open g-file for the rook after castling
Play Nxd4 and develop with tempo

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the bishop. The bishop on g7 is well-placed on the long diagonal, but Black's kingside is permanently weakened. The dark squares around Black's king are full of holes, and castling kingside will leave the king exposed on the open g-file.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's kingside is compromised with the g-pawn gone. The long diagonal is open for Black's bishop, but White can target the weak dark squares around Black's king.
plans: Use the g7 bishop to control the long diagonal
Develop and prepare to castle
Consolidate the extra material

scotch-gambit.annotation: White recaptures the d4 pawn with the knight, centralizing it on a dominant square. The knight on d4 attacks c6, controls e6 and f5, and can jump to various aggressive squares. White has fully recovered the gambit pawn and has a lead in development.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Develop Nc3 or Bg5 for more pressure
Use the centralized knight to support an attack

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black exchanges knights on d4, simplifying the position. Removing White's powerful centralized knight reduces attacking potential. After the queen recaptures, the position is more manageable for Black, though White still has good compensation.
