plans: Simplify the position by trading pieces
Develop the remaining pieces and castle
Use the bishop pair as a long-term asset

scotch-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the queen on the dominant d4 square. The queen is centralized and very active — it controls the d-file, puts pressure on g7, and supports future development. White's lead in development is concrete: the queen is active with Nc3 or Bg5 coming.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Material is close to equal. White has excellent piece activity while Black still needs to castle and connect the rooks. The open d-file and Black's weakened kingside give White lasting pressure.
plans: Develop Nc3 or Bg5 with tempo
Pressure the d-file and Black's weak dark squares
Castle and connect rooks for a powerful middlegame

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black offers a queen trade with Qf6, a practical choice. Trading queens would relieve White's attacking pressure and make the extra pawn more relevant in the endgame. White should avoid the trade to maintain the initiative.
plans: Offer the queen exchange to simplify
If the queen trade happens, the extra pawn becomes meaningful
Develop and castle as quickly as possible
alternatives: O-O — castling directly, keeping the position complex

scotch-gambit.annotation: White retreats the queen to d1, declining the trade. This may look passive, but the queen on d1 supports the d-file and can redeploy. The key point is that White does NOT want to trade queens — the middlegame attack is too strong. From d1 the queen can swing to h5 or other attacking squares.
plans: Develop Nc3 and Bg5 for rapid mobilization
Reposition the queen to h5 or b3 for attacking chances
Castle and bring the rooks to the center
alternatives: Qxf6 — trading queens, but this helps Black consolidate the extra pawn

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety. This is essential — Black has been under pressure in the center and the king needed to evacuate. However, the kingside was weakened earlier by the g-pawn exchange, so the king is not perfectly safe here.
plans: Get the king to safety
Connect the rooks and complete development
Hold the extra pawn and aim for the endgame

scotch-gambit.annotation: White castles as well. Both kings are now safe, but White's position is more harmonious. The rook on f1 is already on a semi-open file, and White's pieces coordinate better. The next phase is rapid development — Nc3, Bg5, and bringing the rooks to the center.
plans: Develop Nc3 to pressure the c4 pawn
Play Bg5 to harass Black's queen
Bring rooks to the d-file and e-file

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black places the rook on the open e-file, a natural and good move. The rook is active on e8 and can create counterplay down the e-file.
plans: Control the e-file for counterplay
Develop the queenside pieces
Support a future pawn advance

scotch-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, attacking the c4 pawn immediately. The knight also controls the key d5 and e4 squares. White's development is now nearly complete, and the pressure on Black's extra pawn is mounting.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: White targets the c4 pawn, which is Black's main material advantage. If White wins it back, the position favors White due to superior piece activity.
plans: Win back the c4 pawn
Follow up with Bg5 or Bf4 for more activity
Double rooks on the d-file

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black defends the c4 pawn with b5, trying to hold onto material. This is ambitious but weakens the queenside significantly. The a6 and c5 squares become weak, and the pawn chain on b5-c4 is fragile.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Black creates a queenside pawn chain b5-c4 to protect the extra pawn, but this creates dark-square weaknesses and leaves the a-pawn backward.
plans: Protect the c4 pawn and maintain material advantage
Develop the bishop to b7 for long diagonal control
Try to trade pieces and reach an endgame
alternatives: Bd7 — developing a piece instead of creating pawn weaknesses
b6 — more modest pawn protection

scotch-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to g5 with tempo, harassing Black's queen on f6. The queen must move, giving White another developing move for free. The bishop on g5 controls the dark squares and can later move to h6 to target the weakened kingside.
plans: Gain a tempo by attacking the queen
Reposition the bishop to h6 to target the kingside
Complete development with Rd1 and seize the initiative

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black moves the queen to d6, out of the bishop's attack. The queen on d6 is reasonably placed — it defends and keeps an eye on the d-file. But the queen has been moved three times already, a sign that White's initiative is real.
plans: Defend the queenside and maintain the extra pawn
Develop the remaining pieces
Look for counterplay on the d-file
alternatives: Qg6 — more active but exposed on the kingside
Qe5 — centralized but blocks the e-file

scotch-gambit.annotation: White brings the queen to f3, a powerful centralizing move. The queen targets the f7 pawn, supports a potential Nd5 jump, and eyes the a8 corner along the diagonal. White's pieces are fully mobilized and coordinated.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: White has full compensation for the pawn with superior piece activity, the bishop pair potential, and pressure on Black's extended queenside pawns.
plans: Threaten Nd5 with devastating effect
Target f7 with queen and potential bishop moves
Double rooks on the d-file for maximum pressure

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to b7, fianchettoing it on the long diagonal. The bishop controls the a8-h1 diagonal and supports the d5 square. Black is finally developing pieces, but White's initiative is still strong.
plans: Control the long diagonal with the bishop
Support d5 and contest White's central control
Complete development and consolidate

scotch-gambit.annotation: White places the rook on the open d-file, completing development. Every single White piece is now active and well-placed. The rook on d1 targets Black's queen on d6 and controls the critical d-file. Combined with Nd5 threats, White's position is extremely dangerous.
plans: Threaten Nd5 forking queen and other pieces
Double rooks on the d-file
Create tactical threats combining queen, bishop, and knight

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black moves the queen to c5, guarding the c4 pawn and getting off the d-file. The queen on c5 is relatively safe but has been moved four times, illustrating the cost of grabbing pawns in the opening.
plans: Guard the c4 pawn from c5
Try to stabilize the position
Develop the a8 rook

scotch-gambit.annotation: The knight leaps to the dominant d5 outpost! This is the move White has been building toward. The knight on d5 is incredibly powerful — it cannot be easily challenged by pawns, it attacks multiple squares, and it supports tactical ideas like Nf6+ or Nc7. Black's position is under tremendous pressure.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: White's knight on d5 is a permanent fixture in the center. Black's pawn structure cannot challenge it, making this outpost worth more than the sacrificed pawn.
plans: Threaten Nf6+ or Nc7 forks
Maintain the knight on d5 as a dominant piece
Combine queen and knight for tactical strikes

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black feels compelled to trade the bishop for the monster knight. Allowing Nd5 to stay would be too dangerous — the threats of Nf6+ and Nc7 are hard to meet. But now White recaptures with the queen, maintaining a centralized and active position.
plans: Eliminate the dangerous knight
Use the bishop on g7 for defense and counterplay
Try to activate the rooks

scotch-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the queen, maintaining a powerful centralized presence. The queen on d5 dominates the board — it controls the long diagonal, the d-file, and keeps pressure on f7 and c4. White has excellent compensation: the queen is incredibly active, the bishop on g5 controls dark squares, and the rooks are well-placed.
plans: Maintain central dominance with the queen
Target f7 and the weakened kingside
Look for tactical shots with Bh6 or Qd7

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black retreats the queen to b6, trying to hold the queenside together. The queen guards b5 and keeps an eye on the b-file. But Black's position remains difficult — the pieces are not well-coordinated and White has all the dynamic chances.
plans: Guard the b5 pawn and queenside
Try to trade queens to ease the pressure
Activate the rooks

scotch-gambit.annotation: White plays the killer move Bh6! The bishop targets the g7 bishop and threatens to remove Black's key defensive piece. After Bxg7 Kxg7, Black's king is completely exposed on the open g-file. This is a classic Scotch Gambit pattern — the dark-squared bishop hunts down the fianchettoed bishop and tears open the kingside.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: After the bishop exchange on g7, Black's kingside will be ripped open. The g-file becomes a highway for White's rook to attack the exposed king.
plans: Exchange the dark-squared bishops to expose Black's king
After Bxg7 Kxg7, use the open g-file for attack
Bring the rook to the g-file for the decisive attack

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black centralizes the queen on f6, defending g7 and offering a queen trade. This is the most resilient defense — the queen guards the critical dark squares and dares White to exchange. If White trades queens, Black's position becomes much more tenable.
plans: Defend g7 and the kingside dark squares
Offer a queen trade to simplify into a safer endgame
Prepare to activate the rooks on the back rank

scotch-gambit.annotation: White pulls the trigger and captures the bishop! This exchange opens the kingside and exposes Black's king. The queen on f6 recaptures, but now there is no dark-squared bishop to guard the king. White can target the dark-square weaknesses with the queen and rooks.
plans: Open the dark squares around Black's king
Switch the queen to target h6, g5, or f4
Use the d-file and potential rook lifts to attack

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the queen on g7. The queen replaces the bishop as the defender of the dark squares, but this is a heavy piece doing a light piece's job. Black's king remains vulnerable, and White can probe with rook lifts and queen maneuvers. The key weakness is that Black's king has no pawn shield on the kingside.
scotch-gambit.pawnStructure: Black's kingside is devastated — no g-pawn, no dark-squared bishop. The h7 pawn is the only shield. White can aim at this weakness with Rd3-g3 or Qd4 ideas.
plans: Defend with the queen on g7
Try to activate the a8 rook
Hold the extra pawn and survive to the endgame

scotch-gambit.annotation: White repositions the queen to d6, maintaining central dominance and eyeing the c7 and a6 pawns. The queen on d6 is extremely well-placed — it pressures multiple weaknesses and restricts Black's rooks. Combined with the rooks on d1 and f1, White's pieces are coordinated for the final assault.
plans: Target the c7 and a7 pawns
Prepare Rd3-g3 or Rd3-h3 rook lifts
Maintain pressure until Black collapses

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black activates the rook on e6, challenging the queen and creating counterplay along the sixth rank. This is a resourceful defense — the rook defends laterally and can swing to g6 or h6 if needed. But White's position remains dominant.
plans: Challenge the queen and fight for activity
Prepare Rg6 or Rh6 for counterplay
Defend the c6 and a6 squares

scotch-gambit.annotation: White shifts the queen to d7, penetrating deep into Black's position. The queen on d7 attacks c7, a7, and threatens to create back-rank problems. Black is being squeezed from all directions. This is the payoff of the Scotch Gambit — dynamic, aggressive play that punishes slow or greedy responses. White's development advantage has been converted into a lasting positional bind.
plans: Threaten Qxc7 winning a pawn
Create back-rank threats with Rd8
Maintain total domination of the position

scotch-gambit.annotation: Black tucks the rook to f8, guarding the back rank and preparing to activate. This is defensive but necessary — Black needs to prevent Rd8+ ideas. The position is very difficult for Black despite the extra pawn. White's queen and rooks dominate the board, and Black's king remains exposed. This final position beautifully illustrates the Scotch Gambit's promise: sacrificed material is recovered with interest through relentless piece activity.
plans: Guard the back rank against Rd8+
Look for any counterplay with the rooks
Try to survive and trade into an endgame

vienna-gambit.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, the starting move of the Vienna Game and its gambit variation. The Vienna Gambit is like a delayed King's Gambit — White will play Nc3 first before pushing f4, getting an extra developing move before the fireworks begin.
plans: Follow up with Nc3 for the Vienna Game
Prepare f4 — the Vienna Gambit pawn sacrifice
alternatives: d4 — Queen's Pawn, completely different character

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black mirrors with e5, entering the Open Game. This is the response the Vienna Gambit is designed to punish. Black occupies the center naturally, but the e5 pawn is about to become a target — except White will be even better prepared than in the King's Gambit.
plans: Hold the center and develop normally
Prepare to meet White's plans

vienna-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to c3 — this is the Vienna Game. The knight supports e4 and controls d5. The real point is what comes next: f4! Unlike the King's Gambit where f4 comes on move 2, the Vienna gets the knight developed first for a tempo advantage.
plans: Play f4 next — the Vienna Gambit
Control d5 and support the center
alternatives: Nf3 — normal development toward Italian or Scotch

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the most natural response. The knight attacks e4 and prepares to castle. This walks right into the Vienna Gambit — after f4, the knight on f6 will need to deal with the opened f-file and White's aggressive intentions.
plans: Attack e4 and develop toward kingside castling
Prepare to meet White's f4 push
alternatives: Nc6 — also natural, supports e5
Bc5 — develops the bishop early

vienna-gambit.annotation: The Vienna Gambit! White pushes f4, challenging Black's e5 pawn just like the King's Gambit — but with Nc3 already developed, a full tempo ahead. The f-file is about to open, and White's king can still maneuver to safety. Black must decide: accept with exf4, or counterstrike with d5.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White strikes at e5, threatening to open the f-file. If Black takes, it mirrors the King's Gambit Accepted but White has Nc3 already — a significant bonus.
plans: Open the f-file for the rook after castling
If exf4, play e5 to chase the knight with tempo
Build a massive center and attack the kingside
alternatives: Bc4 — the Vienna Game without the gambit, quieter

vienna-gambit.annotation: The Falkbeer-style counterstrike! Instead of accepting the gambit, Black plays d5, striking back in the center immediately. This is the most principled and theoretically respected reply. The position becomes extremely sharp — both sides have attacking chances.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: Black challenges the center directly. After exd5 Nxd5, the position opens dramatically. If fxe5, Black gets a strong center with dxe4. Rich tactical possibilities for both sides.
plans: Strike back in the center before White consolidates
After fxe5, play Nxe4 with a centralized knight
Counter White's kingside ambitions with central play
alternatives: exf4 — accepting the gambit, giving White the e5 push with tempo
d6 — solid but passive, giving White a free hand

vienna-gambit.annotation: White captures on e5, winning a pawn and opening the f-file. The pawn on f6 is now attacked by the e5 pawn, so Black must deal with this immediately. White hopes to maintain the extra pawn and use the open f-file for attack.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White has pawns on e4 and e5, forming a powerful central duo. The e5 pawn attacks f6 and controls d6, limiting Black's piece mobility. But Black's d5 pawn challenges e4 directly.
plans: Maintain the e5 pawn as a space advantage
Develop quickly with Nf3, Be2
Use the f-file for kingside pressure
alternatives: exd5 — simpler, leading to open positions

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black captures on e4 with the knight, the sharpest reply. The knight is now centralized on e4, attacking the c3 knight and controlling key squares. Black gives up a pawn but gets incredible piece activity in return. The position enters Frankenstein-Dracula territory — wild and tactical.
plans: Centralize the knight and create tactical threats
Develop Bc5 or Be7 with pressure
Open lines for a direct attack on White's king
alternatives: Ng4 — heading for e3 or f2, also very sharp

vienna-gambit.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, defending e5 and preparing castling. This is the most solid continuation. The knight protects e5 and blocks the f-file temporarily, but once White castles, the f-file will become a powerful attacking lane.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White has a pawn on e5 cramping Black's position. The d5 pawn gives Black central presence, but e5 controls f6 and d6, limiting Black's piece mobility.
plans: Defend e5 and prepare to castle
Develop Be2 and castle quickly
Activate the rook on the f-file

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a solid and flexible choice. The bishop prepares kingside castling and can later reposition to f6 or d6. This prioritizes king safety over immediate aggression.
plans: Castle kingside and connect the rooks
Reposition the bishop to f6 later
Support the knight on e4 and maintain central presence
alternatives: Bc5 — more aggressive, targeting f2
Bb4 — pinning the knight, the Frankenstein-Dracula line

vienna-gambit.annotation: White plays d3, kicking the knight from e4 and solidifying the center. The knight must retreat, and White gains tempo. The d3 pawn also opens the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This is a key moment — White regains space while maintaining e5.
plans: Chase the knight from e4
Develop the bishop along the c1-h6 diagonal
Prepare to castle and activate the rooks

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black captures the knight on c3. The knight on e4 had no good retreat, so taking on c3 is the best practical option. After bxc3, White gets an open b-file and the c3 pawn controls d4. The position is dynamically balanced.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: After bxc3, White has doubled c-pawns but an open b-file for the rook. The c3 pawn controls d4, giving White central influence despite the structural weakness.
plans: Capture the knight and simplify
Develop and castle quickly
Use the extra piece activity

vienna-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the b-pawn, opening the b-file for the rook. The doubled c-pawns look ugly but control d4 firmly. The b-file is now open and White's rook can swing to b1. The position is sharp — White has open lines and compensation.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White has doubled c-pawns but excellent piece activity. The open b-file gives the rook immediate access. The c3 pawn controls d4.
plans: Use the open b-file for rook pressure
Develop Be2 and castle
Maintain e5 and build kingside pressure

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king to safety. This is the priority — the center is open and the king was exposed. The rook on f8 will be useful on the semi-open f-file.
plans: Get the king to safety
Activate the rook on the f-file
Develop the queenside pieces

vienna-gambit.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a flexible square that supports castling and can reposition later. The bishop on e2 covers the g4 and h5 squares, supporting the kingside. White is building up methodically before launching the attack.
plans: Castle and activate the rook on the f-file
Build up with Qe1-Qh4 for a kingside attack
Maintain the e5 pawn as a positional anchor

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, attacking the e5 pawn. This is natural development that pressures White's center. The knight on c6 also supports a future d4 advance and controls key squares.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn
Support d4 or Nb4 ideas
Coordinate pieces for central counterplay

vienna-gambit.annotation: White castles, connecting the rooks and placing the king in safety. The rook on f1 is immediately active on the semi-open f-file. White's position is harmonious and the attack practically plays itself from here.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White's pawn on e5 is the anchor of the position. It controls d6 and f6, cramping Black's pieces. The doubled c-pawns are a minor weakness but piece activity compensates.
plans: Activate the rook on the f-file
Build up with Qe1-Qh4 for a kingside attack
Maintain e5 as a positional anchor

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black challenges the e5 pawn with f6, the most thematic break. This aims to dissolve White's central wedge and open the f-file for Black's rook. However, it also weakens the e6 square and the diagonal toward Black's king.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: Black attacks the e5 chain directly. After exf6 Bxf6, the f-file is fully open for both sides. The position becomes extremely tactical.
plans: Break down White's central pawn chain
Open the f-file for the rook
Activate the bishop pair
alternatives: Bg4 — pinning the knight and maintaining pressure
Bf5 — developing the bishop actively

vienna-gambit.annotation: White captures on f6, opening the e-file and maintaining dynamic play. After Bxf6, the position is wide open with chances for both sides. White's rooks will target the open files.
plans: Open files for the rooks
Target Black's weakened kingside
Build up with Qe1 and Ng5 ideas

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black recaptures with the bishop, keeping the structure intact. The bishop on f6 controls the dark squares and guards the kingside. The f-file is open and both sides can use it. The position is sharp with mutual chances.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: The center is open. Black has d5 as an anchor, White has the c-pawns. The f-file is open for both rooks, creating tactical possibilities.
plans: Maintain the dark-square bishop on f6
Use the open f-file for counterplay
Develop the queenside and coordinate pieces

vienna-gambit.annotation: White repositions the queen to e1, a key multi-purpose move. From e1, the queen can swing to h4 for a kingside attack, or move to e3 for central control. This is a typical Vienna regrouping — the queen stays flexible.
plans: Swing the queen to h4 for a direct kingside attack
Alternatively play Qe3 targeting the center
Coordinate with the bishop and knight

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black pins the knight on f3, creating tactical tension. The pin is annoying for White — the knight is a key defender. If White breaks the pin with h3, it creates kingside weaknesses.
plans: Pin the knight and create tactical pressure
Prepare to double rooks on the f-file
Exchange the bishop for the knight to weaken White's kingside

vienna-gambit.annotation: White boldly moves the queen to h4 despite the pin! The queen creates direct threats against h7 and eyes g5 and f6. White is willing to allow Bxf3 gxf3 because the open g-file would actually HELP the attack. This is the Vienna Gambit spirit — always looking for attack.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: If Black takes on f3, White's g-file opens for attack. The doubled f-pawns are ugly but the open g-file combined with Qh4 creates devastating threats.
plans: Threaten Bxh7+ or Ng5 ideas
If Bxf3, use the open g-file for a mating attack
Keep the pressure on Black's kingside

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black takes the knight, eliminating a key attacker. This is principled — removing the knight reduces White's force. But after gxf3, the g-file blasts open and White's rooks can use it for a devastating attack.
plans: Remove the knight to ease kingside pressure
Accept the open g-file but rely on piece defense
Create counterplay in the center
alternatives: Bh5 — maintaining the pin but retreating
Be6 — redirecting to defend

vienna-gambit.annotation: White recaptures with the g-pawn, and the g-file is wide open! The rook on f1 can now come to g1 with devastating effect. White's pawn structure is damaged but the attacking potential is enormous. The bishop on e2 stays flexible, and the queen on h4 threatens immediate action.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: White's kingside pawns are shattered but the open g-file is a superhighway for attack. Combined with the queen on h4, White has a classic rook-battery setup against Black's king.
plans: Play Kh1 and Rg1 to pile up on the g-file
Combine the queen and rook for a mating attack
Use the f3 pawn to support future pawn breaks

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black places the queen on d6, defending centrally and eyeing both sides. The queen on d6 can help with kingside defense and protects c7 and d5. This is a flexible post — but Black must watch the open g-file carefully.
plans: Defend from the center
Prepare Rae8 for counterplay
Keep the queen flexible for defense
alternatives: Qd7 — connecting the rooks
Bg5 — offering a bishop trade

vienna-gambit.annotation: White tucks the king into the corner with Kh1, a prophylactic move that clears the g-file. Now the rook on f1 can swing to g1 without obstruction. This quiet move dramatically increases White's attacking power.
plans: Clear the g-file for Rg1
Build the battery Qh4 + Rg1
Launch the decisive kingside attack

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black doubles rooks on the e-file, the best counterplay available. The rooks create pressure and can potentially invade on e2 or e1. Black is trying to create threats to distract White from the kingside.
plans: Generate counterplay on the e-file
Threaten Re2 to invade White's position
Coordinate the rooks for maximum pressure

vienna-gambit.annotation: White develops the dark-squared bishop to f4, targeting Black's queen on d6. The queen must move, and White gains another tempo. The bishop on f4 also controls the c7 and e5 squares, restricting Black's pieces. Every White piece is now in the game and attacking.
plans: Attack the queen and gain tempo
Control c7 and e5 with the bishop
Prepare Rg1 for the decisive attack

Try to exchange pieces to relieve pressure
Look for any break to create counterplay

vienna-gambit.annotation: White places the rook on the open g-file — the attack is fully loaded. The rook combines with the queen on h4 and the bishop on f4 to create a massive battery aimed at Black's king. Threats include Bg5, Qg5, or simply doubling rooks on the g-file.

plans: Threaten Bg5 or Qg5 with mating ideas
Double rooks on the g-file with Rag1
Create unstoppable threats on the kingside

Support the kingside with the knight
Prepare c6 to challenge White's center

Clear e2 for potential rook maneuvers
Maintain the pressure while improving piece placement

plans: Defend g7 and the kingside with the knight
Block attacking lines
Support d4 ideas for counterplay

vienna-gambit.annotation: White repositions the bishop to e3, maintaining control of the dark squares while supporting the c3-d3 pawn chain. The bishop on e3 eyes a7 and supports d4 prevention. More importantly, it keeps the tension — White is not rushing but building pressure methodically.
plans: Control the dark squares from e3
Support the pawn chain and prevent d4
Keep options open for Bg5 or Bh6 later

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black strikes with d4, the central break that has been building. This challenges White's pawn chain and opens the center for Black's pieces. The pawn sacrifice opens diagonals and gives Black some counterplay. But White can capture and maintain the initiative.
vienna-gambit.pawnStructure: Black sacrifices the d-pawn to open lines and activate pieces. After cxd4, the center opens and Black's rooks and queen have more room. But White's attacking structure remains intact.
plans: Open the center with d4
Activate the rooks and queen
Create counterplay to distract White from the kingside

vienna-gambit.annotation: White captures with the c-pawn, opening the center. The pawn structure is now more fluid, and White has a strong central pawn on d4. The pawn on e6 is now somewhat exposed, and White can target it or use the open lines for the attack.
plans: Use the central d4 pawn for control
Exploit the open c-file and center
Continue the kingside attack with improved piece coordination

vienna-gambit.annotation: Black captures on d4 with the knight, centralizing it powerfully. The knight on d4 is well-placed but can be challenged by the bishop on e3. This trade opens the position further, and the game enters a critical phase where both sides have active pieces.
plans: Centralize the knight on d4
Create tactical threats in the center
Coordinate pieces for defense and counterplay

Pressure g7 and the kingside dark squares
Maintain the attacking setup with queen and rook

Control the long diagonal
Defend against White's kingside pressure

After the bishop moves, White can continue building the kingside attack with the queen on h4 and rook on g1. The c3 pawn also provides a solid foundation for the center.

Solidify the center
Continue the kingside attack

Maintain the bishop's activity on the diagonal

danish-gambit.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, claiming central space and preparing for one of the most audacious gambits in chess. The Danish Gambit will sacrifice not one but TWO pawns for a devastating lead in development.
plans: Prepare d4 to open the center

danish-gambit.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, occupying the center and opening the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This is the most natural reply, but Black is walking into dangerous territory — the Danish Gambit thrives against this classical setup.

Prepare to meet White's central advance
alternatives: c5 — the Sicilian, avoiding gambit territory entirely

danish-gambit.annotation: White strikes in the center immediately! By pushing d4, we challenge Black's e5 pawn and begin to open lines. This is the first step of the Danish Gambit sequence — we WANT Black to capture. Every pawn Black takes opens another line for our pieces.
plans: After exd4, play c3 to offer a second pawn
Open the center for rapid piece development
alternatives: Nf3 — the quieter Italian/Scotch approach

danish-gambit.annotation: Black captures the pawn — the most natural and principled response. Black is up a pawn and the center is open. But this is exactly what White wants. The d-file is now open and White's next move will offer yet another pawn sacrifice.

Develop quickly to consolidate

danish-gambit.annotation: The first real gambit move! White offers a SECOND pawn with c3, daring Black to grab it. The idea is brilliant: if Black takes on c3, the b2 pawn will be next, but then White's bishops will be firing on all cylinders. White is playing for total piece domination.
danish-gambit.pawnStructure: White is about to sacrifice a second pawn. The center will be wide open, and White's development advantage will be enormous if Black keeps taking.
plans: After dxc3, offer the b2 pawn as well with Bc4

danish-gambit.annotation: Black takes the second pawn! Greed is tempting, and the material advantage is real. But Black has spent three moves just capturing pawns while developing ZERO pieces. Meanwhile, White is about to unleash a development hurricane.
danish-gambit.pawnStructure: Black is up two pawns but has no development. The c3 pawn is about to be captured again, and White's piece activity will be overwhelming.
plans: Grab more material and try to survive
Develop quickly to consolidate the two-pawn lead
alternatives: d5 — the best practical response, giving back a pawn to slow White down

Aim both bishops at Black's kingside
Castle quickly and pile up on the open files

Try to get pieces out as fast as possible

Play Nc3 or Qb3 to increase pressure on f7
Launch a direct kingside attack before Black can develop

plans: Attack e4 and develop quickly
Prepare to castle and consolidate
Try to catch up in development
alternatives: d5 — returning a pawn to break White's center
d6 — solid but slow

plans: Chase the knight and gain more development time
Follow up with Nf3 and O-O for rapid mobilization
Keep the initiative and prevent Black from castling

Develop the bishop and prepare to castle
Try to stabilize the position

plans: Threaten Qxg7, winning the rook
Build up with Nf3 and O-O next
Create a mating attack on the kingside

Castle kingside quickly
Try to develop and consolidate

The development advantage is enormous.
plans: Castle next move and complete development
Support e5 and prepare Ng5 or Nd4
Maintain the crushing development lead

Castle and bring the rook into the attack
Prepare Nc3 to develop the last minor piece

Develop and prepare to castle
Coordinate the knights for defense

Maintain e5 and look for Nc3 or Ng5

Develop the bishop after the pawn exchange
Try to castle as quickly as possible

Prepare Rad1 to control the d-file

Develop the remaining pieces
Try to stabilize before White's attack crashes through

Prepare to open the position with exd6
Coordinate all pieces for a decisive attack

Prepare to castle

Use the d-file and e-file for rook pressure

Castle as soon as possible

Use the open a-file if needed
Continue the kingside attack

Activate the rook on the f-file
Defend f7 and try to consolidate

Maintain maximum pressure on the kingside

After Rxf7, use the open f-file and weak king

Threaten Ne6 forking queen and attacking key squares

Threaten Nxc7 winning material
Continue the attack with the bishop and queen

Centralize the queen for defense
Try to coordinate the remaining pieces

Maintain the knight deep in Black's camp

Generate counterplay on the c-file

Threaten Nxe7 winning material

Simplify the position slightly
Try to hold the remaining position

This is the Danish Gambit at its finest — material was sacrificed for time, development, and initiative, and now those advantages are decisive.

Centralize the queen for defense
Block the e-file

danish-gambit.annotation: White invades the seventh rank with the rook — the classic finishing blow! The rook on d7 attacks the bishop on e7, threatens to capture on b7, and completely dominates the position from the seventh rank. A rook on the seventh is one of the most powerful weapons in chess, and here it seals the game. Black is being squeezed from all directions, with the queen on h3, bishop on b2, and rook on d7 all working in perfect harmony. This final position is the reward of the Danish Gambit: two pawns sacrificed for a position where every White piece is maximally active and the attack is unstoppable.
plans: Dominate from the seventh rank
Attack b7 and e7 simultaneously
Create mating threats with the queen and rook

marshall-attack.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, controlling the center and opening lines for the bishop and queen. This is the most popular first move in chess, leading to open tactical positions.
plans: Control the center and develop pieces rapidly
Prepare for an open game with tactical chances

marshall-attack.annotation: Black mirrors White's central advance, establishing equal control of the center. This is the classical response, leading to open games where both sides have active piece play.
plans: Maintain central equality
Develop knights to f6 and c6 for active piece play

marshall-attack.annotation: White develops the knight to attack the e5 pawn while controlling the center. This is the most natural developing move, preparing for the Ruy Lopez, Italian Game, or Scotch Game depending on White's next moves.
plans: Attack e5 and prepare Bb5 for the Ruy Lopez
Develop pieces with tempo

marshall-attack.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with the knight, developing a piece to its most natural square. The knight on c6 controls important central squares and prepares for various defense systems.
plans: Defend e5 and develop harmoniously
Prepare ...a6 and ...b5 for the Marshall Attack setup

marshall-attack.annotation: The Ruy Lopez! White pins the knight that defends e5, creating long-term pressure. This is one of the oldest and most respected openings in chess. The bishop on b5 threatens to capture on c6, doubling Black's pawns and undermining the defense of e5.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: The center remains symmetrical with pawns on e4 and e5. The Ruy Lopez aims to exert slow pressure and maintain a slight initiative.
plans: Maintain pressure on the c6 knight and e5 pawn
Build a strong center and prepare d4

marshall-attack.annotation: The Morphy Defense — Black asks the bishop an important question: retreat or capture? This move gains tempo and prepares ...b5 to push the bishop further back. Almost every top-level Ruy Lopez game features this move.
plans: Force the bishop to decide its future
Prepare ...b5 and ...Bb7 for queenside expansion

marshall-attack.annotation: White retreats the bishop, maintaining the pin on the c6 knight. The bishop on a4 still puts pressure on the queenside and can later retreat to b3 where it eyes the f7 pawn. White preserves the bishop pair and keeps the tension.
plans: Maintain the pin and pressure on c6
Retreat to b3 after ...b5 to target f7

marshall-attack.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight, attacking the e4 pawn and preparing to castle. This is the most flexible response, delaying the decision of where to put the dark-squared bishop. The knight on f6 is Black's most active minor piece.
plans: Attack e4 and prepare kingside castling
Set up the Marshall Attack with ...b5 and ...d5

marshall-attack.annotation: White castles, securing the king and connecting the rooks. Castling early is essential in the Ruy Lopez to prepare Re1 supporting the e4 pawn. The king is safe on g1 and the rook will soon join the central battle.
plans: Play Re1 to support the e4 pawn
Prepare d3 or d4 to maintain the center

marshall-attack.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a solid square that prepares castling. In the Marshall Attack, Black deliberately plays this modest bishop move rather than the more ambitious ...Bc5, saving the dynamic ideas for after castling and ...d5.
plans: Castle kingside next move
Prepare the Marshall pawn sacrifice with ...b5 and ...d5

marshall-attack.annotation: White places the rook behind the e4 pawn, the most natural square. The rook defends e4 and prepares for the eventual opening of the e-file. This is a standard Ruy Lopez move that strengthens White's central control.
plans: Support e4 and prepare d4 central advance
Keep the center stable while completing development

marshall-attack.annotation: Black pushes the queenside pawns, forcing the bishop back and gaining space. This move is essential to the Marshall Attack — it prepares the d5 pawn break by first securing the queenside. The b5 advance also opens future possibilities for the light-squared bishop.
plans: Force Bb3 and then play ...d5 for the Marshall sacrifice
Gain queenside space and open the diagonal for the bishop

marshall-attack.annotation: White retreats the bishop to b3, a strong diagonal where it targets the f7 pawn. The bishop is well-placed here, though it will eventually become a target after Black's ...d5 advance. This is the standard retreat in the Ruy Lopez.
plans: Target the f7 pawn from the a2-g8 diagonal
Prepare c3 and d4 for a central pawn push

marshall-attack.annotation: Black castles, completing kingside development and setting the stage for the Marshall Attack. With the king safe, Black can now launch the aggressive ...d5 pawn sacrifice. The king on g8 will be surprisingly safe despite Black's coming kingside attack plans — it's White's king that will be in danger.
plans: Launch the Marshall Attack with ...d5 on the next move
Sacrifice the e-pawn for a ferocious kingside attack

marshall-attack.annotation: White prepares d4 to build an ideal pawn center. The c3 pawn supports the d4 advance and prevents any knight incursions to d4 or b4. This is standard Ruy Lopez technique — solidifying the center before launching any action.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: White prepares the c3-d4 pawn duo, aiming for maximum central control. This structure is the backbone of White's Ruy Lopez strategy.
plans: Play d4 to open the center with advantage
Build a strong pawn center and develop the queenside

marshall-attack.annotation: The Marshall Attack begins! Black sacrifices a pawn with this bold central thrust, breaking open the position before White can consolidate with d4. Frank Marshall kept this move secret for years, saving it for a crucial game against Capablanca in 1918. The idea is to open lines toward White's king and generate a ferocious attack.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: Black opens the center at the cost of a pawn. The resulting open lines and diagonals favor Black's pieces, which are aimed at White's king.
plans: Sacrifice the e5 pawn for open lines and piece activity
Launch a kingside attack with ...Bd6, ...Qh4, and ...Bg4
Use the open e-file and diagonals for the attack
alternatives: d6 — the solid Chigorin variation, avoiding the gambit

marshall-attack.annotation: White captures, the most principled response. Declining with d3 or d4 gives Black easy equality. By accepting the pawn, White takes on the challenge of defending with an extra pawn but facing a powerful attack.
plans: Win a pawn and try to defend accurately
Prepare to weather Black's coming initiative

marshall-attack.annotation: Black recaptures with the knight, centralizing it powerfully on d5. The knight on d5 is a monster — it controls key squares and can reroute to f4 or f6 depending on the position. More importantly, the e5 pawn is now hanging, and Black is happy to sacrifice it.
plans: Allow Nxe5, then launch the attack after ...Nxe5 Rxe5 c6
Centralize pieces and aim for the kingside

marshall-attack.annotation: White captures the e5 pawn, winning material. However, this is exactly what Black wants — the open e-file and active piece play will generate a powerful attack against White's king. White has an extra pawn but must survive the coming storm.
plans: Hold the extra pawn and defend accurately
Try to simplify and reach a safe endgame

marshall-attack.annotation: Black recaptures, exchanging knights and opening the e-file completely. The e-file is now a highway for Black's rook, and the knight on e5 is temporarily powerful before White chases it away. Black's compensation for the pawn is becoming clear.
plans: Use the open e-file for the rook
Prepare ...c6, ...Bd6, and ...Qh4 for the kingside attack

marshall-attack.annotation: White captures the knight, clearing the e-file but placing the rook on an exposed square. The rook on e5 will become a target for Black's pieces, particularly after ...Bd6 and ...c6. White has won two pawns but Black's attack is gathering steam.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: Black is down two pawns but has massive compensation. The open center and Black's superior piece activity create dangerous attacking chances.
plans: Use the active rook while defending the king
Try to return material at the right moment to defuse the attack

marshall-attack.annotation: Black supports the d5 knight and prepares to drive the knight to more active squares. The c6 pawn also opens the diagonal for the queen to reach the kingside via d6-h2 or via h4. This quiet move is the key to the Marshall — it solidifies the position before the storm.
plans: Support the knight and prepare ...Bd6 to attack the rook
Clear the d8-h4 diagonal for the queen
Build the kingside attack step by step

marshall-attack.annotation: White advances the d-pawn, gaining central space and opening lines for the bishop. This is the most common continuation, building a strong center and trying to make the extra pawns count. However, it also opens the position for Black's pieces.
plans: Establish a strong center and develop the queenside
Use the extra pawns to gain a long-term advantage
alternatives: d3 — a more cautious approach, keeping things closed

marshall-attack.annotation: The bishop attacks the rook on e5, forcing it to make a decision. From d6, the bishop also eyes the kingside, particularly the h2 pawn. This is a critical developing move that both attacks and prepares the kingside assault.
plans: Drive the rook from e5
Set up ...Qh4 for a direct attack on the kingside
Use the bishop's diagonal toward h2 for mating threats

marshall-attack.annotation: White retreats the rook to the safe e1 square, maintaining the open e-file. The rook on e1 defends the first rank and keeps an eye on Black's pieces. Other retreats like Re2 are possible but block the second rank.
plans: Keep the e-file under control
Develop the queenside pieces and consolidate
alternatives: Re2 — keeping the rook active but blocking the second rank

marshall-attack.annotation: The queen swoops to h4, launching the direct kingside attack. This is the signature move of the Marshall Attack — the queen threatens Qxh2 mate and coordinates with the bishop on d6. White must defend carefully to survive the onslaught.
plans: Threaten Qxh2 checkmate
Coordinate with ...Bg4 to add more attackers
Build a devastating kingside assault

marshall-attack.annotation: White is forced to weaken the kingside to stop Qxh2 mate. The g3 move creates dark-square weaknesses around the king but is necessary. Now Black shifts the queen and brings more pieces into the attack.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: White's kingside is permanently weakened by g3. The dark squares f2, g3, and h2 become potential targets for Black's pieces.
plans: Block the mate threat on h2
Try to develop and use the extra pawns

marshall-attack.annotation: The queen repositions to h3, maintaining the kingside pressure while targeting g2 and preparing to coordinate with ...Bg4. From h3, the queen also keeps an eye on the d3 and e3 squares, supporting future pawn advances.
plans: Prepare ...Bg4 to add another attacker
Maintain pressure on g2 and the kingside
Set up tactical ideas with the bishop pair

marshall-attack.annotation: White develops the knight to protect the kingside and prepare to reroute it to f1 or f3 for defensive purposes. The knight on d2 is a key defender, covering f1 and e4, though it temporarily blocks the dark-squared bishop.
plans: Reroute the knight to f1 or f3 for king defense
Try to untangle and activate the queenside pieces

marshall-attack.annotation: The bishop enters the attack, targeting the f3 square and cutting off potential knight retreats. This is a key attacking idea in the Marshall — the bishop on g4 pins nothing but creates tactical threats and supports the queen's kingside assault.
plans: Add another piece to the kingside attack
Threaten ...Bf3 to weaken White's king shelter
Coordinate bishop and queen against g2

marshall-attack.annotation: White retreats the queen to f1 to defend the kingside. This is a common defensive resource — the queen on f1 covers f2 and helps shield the king. However, the queen is passive here and White's pieces lack coordination.
plans: Defend the kingside with the queen
Try to reroute pieces for active defense

marshall-attack.annotation: Black brings the last rook into the attack via the open e-file. Both rooks are now aimed at White's position, and the combined force of queen, two bishops, and two rooks creates enormous pressure. This is the full Marshall Attack in its glory.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: Black remains down a pawn but has total control of the open e-file and superior piece coordination. The attack is worth far more than the material deficit.
plans: Double rooks on the e-file for maximum pressure
Threaten ...Re2 to invade the second rank
Keep building the attack until White collapses

marshall-attack.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, trying to block the e-file and defend. The knight on f3 is a key defensive piece, covering h2, e1, and d4. White is trying to consolidate but Black's attacking force is formidable.
plans: Block the e-file with the knight
Defend the kingside and try to simplify

marshall-attack.annotation: Black lifts the rook to e6, a multipurpose move. The rook can swing to f6 or h6 to join the kingside attack, or stay on e6 to support the other rook. This rook lift is a typical attacking technique that adds tremendous firepower.
plans: Swing the rook to f6 or h6 for a kingside attack
Double rooks on the e-file with ...Rae8
Create tactical threats involving the rook and queen

marshall-attack.annotation: White captures the rook, trying to simplify and reduce the attacking force. Trading a pair of rooks is usually good for the defender, but Black recaptures with threats and maintains the initiative.
plans: Simplify the position to ease the defense
Use the open file for counter-pressure after the exchange

marshall-attack.annotation: Black recaptures with the f-pawn, opening the f-file for the remaining rook. The f-file is now a deadly highway aimed directly at White's king. The pawn on e6 also supports a potential ...d5-d4-d3 advance and keeps the center fluid.
marshall-attack.pawnStructure: The f-file is now open, giving Black's rook direct access to White's king. The e6 pawn supports future central advances.
plans: Use the open f-file to attack White's king
Prepare ...Rf8 to swing the rook into the attack
Advance ...e5-e4 to open more lines

marshall-attack.annotation: White develops the bishop to d2, trying to complete development and connect the rooks. The bishop is somewhat passive here but covers some important squares. White is still struggling to find a safe setup against Black's continuing initiative.
plans: Complete development and try to consolidate
Prepare Bc1 or Be3 for better placement

marshall-attack.annotation: Black exchanges the bishop for the knight, removing a key defender and opening lines toward the king. After this capture, White's kingside is severely weakened and the queen on h3 eyes the g2 square with renewed menace.
plans: Remove a key defender of the kingside
Open lines for the queen and rook to attack g2

marshall-attack.annotation: White recaptures with the queen, the only reasonable option. The queen on f3 defends g2 and guards against immediate threats. But now Black's rook enters the attack on the f-file with devastating effect.
plans: Defend g2 and try to hold the position
Seek piece trades to ease the pressure

marshall-attack.annotation: The queen delivers check from h1, forcing White's king to the corner. This check is not just flashy — it forces the king to an inferior square and sets up further tactical ideas. Black's attack is relentless.
plans: Force the king to an awkward square
Follow up with ...Rf8 to bring the rook into the attack

marshall-attack.annotation: The king steps to f2, the only escape square. The king is exposed on f2 and vulnerable to checks and tactical ideas along the open files. White's extra pawns mean nothing if the king cannot find safety.
plans: Try to escape the checks and consolidate
Regroup the pieces for defense

marshall-attack.annotation: A stunning bishop sacrifice! Black tears open the king's shelter, destroying the g3 pawn and exposing the king to a raging attack. After the recapture, the queen and rook will have devastating access to the king. This is the kind of brilliant tactical shot that makes the Marshall Attack legendary.
plans: Destroy the king's pawn shelter
Create mating threats with the queen and rook
Force the king into the open

marshall-attack.annotation: White must capture the bishop. The kingside is now completely shattered with the open h-file and weakened g3 pawn. Black's attack is reaching its climax with the queen and rook ready to deliver the final blow.
plans: Recapture and try to defend the exposed king
Hope Black's attack runs out of steam

marshall-attack.annotation: The queen checks on g2, forcing the king to retreat further. Black's attack flows naturally — every move comes with a threat. The king is being driven into the open where it will face a mating attack from the queen and rook.
plans: Force the king into the open
Set up a mating net with the rook on the f-file

marshall-attack.annotation: The king stumbles to e3, the only square available. The king is completely exposed in the center of the board, a hopeless situation. White's extra pawns are meaningless when the king is caught in a mating net.
plans: Try to survive the immediate threats
The position is objectively lost for White

marshall-attack.annotation: The queen continues the relentless checks, giving check from g1 and cutting off the king's escape. The king is being herded into a mating net. This is the Marshall Attack at its most beautiful — Black's attack flows move after move with irresistible force.
plans: Continue checking the king toward the center
Set up a decisive combination with the rook

albin-countergambit.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, the starting move for most positional openings. This controls the center and prepares the Queen's Gambit with c4. White aims for a solid, strategic game.
plans: Follow up with c4 for the Queen's Gambit
Build a strong center and develop methodically

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black mirrors the central advance, occupying the center symmetrically. This is the most classical response to 1.d4, maintaining equal central control and preparing to challenge White's plans directly.
plans: Contest the center and prepare development
Meet c4 with the aggressive ...e5 counter-gambit

albin-countergambit.annotation: The Queen's Gambit — White attacks the d5 pawn with the c-pawn, offering a pawn sacrifice to gain central control. This is one of the oldest and most respected opening moves, aiming to lure the d5 pawn away from the center.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: White's c4 and d4 pawns form the classic Queen's Gambit duo, pressuring Black's center and fighting for space.
plans: Lure the d5 pawn away from the center
Gain central dominance after cxd5

albin-countergambit.annotation: The Albin Counter-Gambit! Instead of defending the d5 pawn, Black strikes back aggressively in the center with ...e5. This bold move sacrifices a pawn but aims to seize the initiative with an advanced passed d-pawn. The Albin is a surprise weapon that can catch unprepared opponents off guard.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: Black offers the e5 pawn to create an advanced d-pawn after the exchange. The resulting d4 pawn will be a powerful passed pawn deep in White's territory.
plans: Sacrifice the e-pawn for a powerful d4 advance
Create an advanced passed pawn and active piece play
Set traps including the famous Lasker Trap
alternatives: e6 — the solid Queen's Gambit Declined
dxc4 — the Queen's Gambit Accepted

albin-countergambit.annotation: White accepts the gambit, capturing the e5 pawn. This is the most natural response, but it allows Black's d-pawn to advance to d4 where it becomes a serious nuisance. Declining with e3 or Nf3 leads to quieter positions where Black has easy equality.
plans: Try to hold the extra pawn and neutralize Black's initiative
Develop carefully while dealing with the d4 pawn
alternatives: e3 — declining the gambit, leading to a solid but unambitious position

albin-countergambit.annotation: The point of the Albin! Black advances the d-pawn to d4, creating a powerful passed pawn in the heart of White's position. This pawn is a thorn that disrupts White's natural development. The d4 pawn controls c3 and e3, making it difficult for White to develop normally.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: Black's d4 pawn is an advanced passed pawn that cramps White's position. It controls c3 and e3, preventing normal development patterns.
plans: Use the d4 pawn to disrupt White's development
Develop pieces to support the d-pawn's advance to d3
Create tactical threats using the pawn as a battering ram

albin-countergambit.annotation: White tries to eliminate the dangerous d4 pawn immediately. This is the most common response but it walks directly into the famous Lasker Trap! After ...Bb4+ and ...dxe3, Black gets a hidden passed pawn on e3 that can cause devastating tactical damage.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: White tries to challenge the d4 pawn head-on. However, after the exchange, Black may end up with an equally dangerous passed pawn on e3.
plans: Eliminate the d4 pawn and develop normally
Open the center and try to use the extra pawn
alternatives: Nf3 — the main line, developing while blocking the d-pawn's advance
a3 — a quiet approach preventing ...Bb4+

albin-countergambit.annotation: A critical intermezzo! Black checks with the bishop before capturing on e3. This forces White to deal with the check, and the most natural response Bd2 walks into the Lasker Trap. The bishop on b4 is aggressively placed, pinning potential defenders.
plans: Force White to block the check, setting up the Lasker Trap
Develop with tempo and maintain the initiative

albin-countergambit.annotation: White blocks with the bishop, the most natural move but it falls into the Lasker Trap! After ...dxe3, the pawn on e3 threatens a devastating discovered attack. White should prefer Nd2 here, which avoids the trap entirely.
plans: Block the check and try to simplify
Develop and hope to neutralize the d-pawn
alternatives: Nd2 — the correct move, avoiding the Lasker Trap entirely

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black captures on e3, and now the hidden trap is set. The e3 pawn threatens to advance to e2, forking the king and queen, or to capture on f2 or d2. This is the Lasker Trap — the pawn on e3 is far more dangerous than it appears at first glance.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: The e3 pawn is a devastating infiltrator. It threatens to advance or capture in multiple directions, creating tactical chaos in White's camp.
plans: Threaten ...exf2+ winning the queen
Threaten ...exd2+ with a discovered attack
Force White into an inferior position

albin-countergambit.annotation: White captures the bishop, trying to win material and remove Black's active piece. But this is the losing move in the Lasker Trap! White should instead play fxe3 to eliminate the dangerous pawn, even though it weakens the king.
plans: Win the bishop and try to deal with the e3 pawn
alternatives: fxe3 — the best defense, accepting a weakened king position but eliminating the threat

albin-countergambit.annotation: The Lasker Trap springs! The pawn advances with check, and White's king must move since the f2 pawn is captured. The king must go to e2 or d2, and in either case Black has a devastating follow-up. This trap has claimed countless victims at all levels of play.
plans: Force the king to move and follow up with ...fxg1=N+
The underpromotion to knight is the key tactical idea

albin-countergambit.annotation: The king moves to e2, the only legal move that doesn't lose immediately. The king is exposed in the center, and now comes the brilliant tactical point of the entire Lasker Trap.
plans: Try to survive with the exposed king
Hope to consolidate with the extra piece

albin-countergambit.annotation: The crown jewel of the Lasker Trap — an underpromotion to a knight with check! Promoting to a queen would not give check, but the pawn on g1 forks the king and the rook on h1. This is one of the most beautiful tactical ideas in opening theory. Black wins the exchange and has a superior position.
plans: Win the rook on h1 with the knight fork
Maintain a winning material advantage
Continue developing and converting the advantage

albin-countergambit.annotation: The king retreats, trying to escape the knight's fork. After Black takes the rook, Black will have a rook and pawn for two minor pieces — a roughly equal material balance but with a much better position due to White's discoordinated pieces.
plans: Retreat and try to salvage the position
Develop pieces and hope for compensation

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black continues the assault with a queen check, keeping the initiative alive. The queen on h4 threatens the king and prevents White from consolidating. Black maintains enormous pressure despite the unusual material distribution.
plans: Keep the king exposed with checks
Coordinate the queen and knight against the vulnerable king
Prevent White from castling or developing normally

albin-countergambit.annotation: White blocks the check with the g-pawn, further weakening the kingside. The king's position is a shambles — dark squares are weakened, development is lagging, and Black's pieces are actively placed. White is in serious trouble.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: White's kingside is shattered with pawns on g3 and the absence of the f-pawn. The dark squares around the king are chronically weak.
plans: Block the check and try to develop
The kingside is permanently weakened

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black repositions the queen to e4 with check, centralizing it powerfully and threatening the rook on h1. The queen on e4 controls key squares and keeps the pressure on White's exposed king. Every Black piece is finding active squares naturally.
plans: Maintain central dominance with the queen
Target the h1 rook and prevent White from castling
Bring more pieces into the attack

albin-countergambit.annotation: White blocks with the bishop, the only reasonable defense. The position is grim for White — the king is stuck in the center, development is severely lagging, and Black has a powerful initiative with the queen and knight actively placed.
plans: Block the check and try to develop the knight
Aim to castle queenside eventually

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black's knight hops to f3, an incredibly strong square deep in White's position. The knight on f3 is protected by the queen on e4 and attacks multiple key squares. White's position is close to collapse with the knight embedded so deeply.
plans: Dominate White's position with the embedded knight
Threaten ...Nxh2 winning more material
Prepare to bring the other pieces into the game

albin-countergambit.annotation: White finally develops the knight, trying to challenge Black's central control. However, the position is already too compromised. White's pieces are tangled and the king remains vulnerable in the center.
plans: Develop and try to consolidate
Challenge Black's queen on e4

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black recovers the gambit pawn while maintaining a powerful position. The queen on e5 is centralized and active, threatening multiple squares. Black now has material equality with a far superior position — the knight on f3 and active queen guarantee a lasting advantage.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: Material is now roughly equal but Black's piece activity is overwhelming. White's shattered kingside and undeveloped pieces make the position nearly hopeless.
plans: Consolidate the advantage with development
Use the active pieces to create mating threats
Keep the White king pinned in the center

albin-countergambit.annotation: White develops the knight to f3 (Black's knight having moved to the captured e5 pawn), trying to add a defender and contest the center. But with the king exposed and pieces poorly coordinated, White's defensive task is nearly impossible.
plans: Develop and try to castle quickly
Contest the central squares

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black retreats the queen to f6, maintaining pressure on the f3 knight and the overall position. The queen is still active from f6, eyeing the kingside and preventing White from achieving any stability. Black can now focus on bringing the remaining pieces into play.
plans: Maintain pressure on the kingside
Develop ...Nc6 and ...Bg4 to increase the attack
Look for tactical shots against the exposed king

albin-countergambit.annotation: White activates the queen, targeting the b7 pawn and trying to create some counterplay. This is White's best practical chance — attacking Black's undeveloped queenside while hoping to gain time for consolidation.
plans: Attack b7 and create counter-threats
Try to exchange queens to ease the defensive burden

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, defending b4 and adding another piece to the game. The knight on c6 is well-placed, supporting potential ...Nd4 or ...Nb4 invasions. Black's development is catching up while maintaining the initiative.
plans: Complete development with ...Bg4 and ...O-O-O
Prepare ...Nd4 to invade White's position
Maintain the pressure on the center and kingside

albin-countergambit.annotation: White captures the b7 pawn, trying to grab material while Black is still developing. This is a risky decision — the queen is now far from the kingside and may become trapped. White's greed will be punished if Black can mobilize quickly.
plans: Win a pawn and hope to survive
Try to escape with the queen while holding the position

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop with tempo, pinning the f3 knight against the king. The bishop on g4 is perfectly placed, adding to the pressure on White's position. Black now threatens ...Bxf3 to destroy White's only good defender.
plans: Pin the f3 knight and threaten to capture it
Complete development and castle queenside
Increase pressure on the exposed king

albin-countergambit.annotation: White retreats the queen to b5, eyeing the c6 knight and trying to create some counter-threats. The queen on b5 is somewhat active but far from the kingside where the real battle is happening. White is hoping to survive by creating complications.
plans: Pin the c6 knight and create counter-threats
Try to survive the middlegame with active queen play

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black activates the rook on the b-file, chasing White's queen and seizing the open file. The rook on b8 attacks the queen and prepares to infiltrate White's position. Black's pieces are all working harmoniously while White's are scattered.
plans: Chase the queen and seize the b-file
Prepare to castle queenside or continue the middlegame attack
Coordinate all pieces for the final assault

albin-countergambit.annotation: White retreats the queen again, losing more tempo. The queen on a4 is out of play, and White has wasted several moves with the queen while Black has been developing. This is the price of greed in the Albin Counter-Gambit.
plans: Try to regroup and survive
Look for any tactical chance to simplify

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black castles kingside, placing the king in safety and connecting the rooks. The rook on f8 is now ready to join the attack, and Black's position is completely coordinated. The contrast between the two sides is striking — Black has a safe king and active pieces while White's king is still stuck in the center.
albin-countergambit.pawnStructure: Black has recovered the gambit pawn and has a superior pawn structure. White's kingside is weakened and the position is difficult to defend.
plans: Bring the f8 rook into play
Launch a decisive attack with all pieces coordinated
Convert the positional and developmental advantage

albin-countergambit.annotation: White finally develops a rook, placing it on the d-file. However, this comes far too late — Black's lead in development and piece activity is decisive. White's position is difficult to save at this point.
plans: Try to activate the rook and create some counter-chances
Defend the king from central attacks

albin-countergambit.annotation: Black places the rook on the open e-file, targeting the exposed king. With both rooks, the queen, two knights, and the bishop all active, Black's position is overwhelming. The threat of ...Rxe2+ is in the air, and White's defenses are crumbling. The Albin Counter-Gambit has delivered its full promise — dynamic piece play and a lasting initiative that converts into a winning attack.
plans: Target the king along the e-file
Threaten ...Rxe2+ to win material
Finish the game with coordinated piece play

pro-naroditsky-jobava-london.annotation: The starting move of the Jobava London System. We're claiming the center and preparing to develop our pieces aggressively. Naroditsky chooses d4 because the Jobava setup creates immediate tactical threats while avoiding the massive theory of mainline Queen's Gambit or Nimzo-Indian positions.
Black's most natural response, fighting for central control. By playing d5, Black mirrors our central pawn and prevents an immediate e4.

The key move that defines the Jobava London! Instead of the traditional London move Nf3, we develop the knight to c3 where it pressures d5 and prepares the Nb5 jump targeting c7. The knight on c3 also supports a future e4 pawn break.
plans: Nb5 threatening Nc7+ fork
Support e4 break with f3
Coordinate with Bf4 to control dark squares

pro-naroditsky-jobava-london.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, defending d5 and preparing to castle. From here, the game branches into many critical variations depending on Black's next moves.
alternatives: c5 — immediately challenges the center
e6 — solid French-like setup
g6 — fianchetto with sharp play
Bf5 — develops the bishop but allows the f3-g4 bishop hunt
c6 — Slav-like setup

pro-naroditsky-jobava-london.annotation: Completing the Jobava London setup! The bishop comes to f4 BEFORE playing e3, keeping it active outside the pawn chain. Together, Nc3 and Bf4 create a powerful duo — the knight threatens Nb5 hitting c7, while the bishop already eyes that same square.
plans: Nb5 + Bf4 pressure on c7
e3 followed by Bd3 and O-O
Against g6: h4-h5 kingside attack
Against Bf5: f3-g4-h4 bishop hunt

pro-naroditsky-jobava-london.annotation: Black chooses the solid e6 setup, bolstering d5. However, this move blocks in the light-squared bishop and does nothing to prevent our Nb5 jump!
alternatives: g6 — leads to the sharp h4 attack
Bf5 — allows the f3-g4 bishop hunt
c5 — challenges the center directly
c6 — Slav setup, solid but passive
a6 — prevents Nb5 but wastes tempo

pro-naroditsky-jobava-london.annotation: The signature move of the Jobava London against e6! The knight jumps to b5 threatening Nc7+ forking the king and rook. Black is forced to play the awkward Na6. Naroditsky shows that Black's knight on a6 will take several tempi to reroute, giving White a lasting initiative.
